Step 1.  Exterior–angle language  
Orient the simple 300-gon counter-clockwise.  
At every vertex let  

 ε = 180° − θ  

be the (signed) exterior angle, where θ is the interior angle at that vertex.
For such a polygon  

 Σ ε = 360°. (1)

Step 2.  What values can ε take?  
• If θ = 90° (a right angle) then ε = +90°.  
• If 0° < θ < 180° and θ ≠ 90° (a non-right convex angle) then 0° < ε < 180°.  
• If 180° < θ < 360° (a reflex angle) then −180° < ε < 0°.  

In particular, every non-right vertex contributes more than −180° to the sum (1).

Step 3.  An inequality for the number of right angles  
Let k be the number of interior right angles.
Equation (1) can be written as  

 360° = k·90° + Σ ε_other.        (2)

Because each of the remaining 300 − k exterior angles exceeds −180°,  

 Σ ε_other > −180°·(300 − k).     (3)

Insert (3) into (2):

 360 > 90k − 180(300 − k)  
 360 > 90k − 54000 + 180k  
 54360 > 270k  

 k < 54360⁄270 = 201⅓.

Hence  

 k ≤ 201.                         (4)

So at most 201 interior angles can be right angles.

Step 4.  Showing that 201 can actually be achieved  
Choose the 300 exterior angles as follows  

 • 201 vertices: ε = +90°  
 • 98 vertices: ε = −179°  
 • 1 vertex:  ε = −139°.

Their sum is  

 201·90 + 98(−179) + (−139)  
 = 18090 − 17542 − 139  
 = 360°,

matching (1).  
Every ε is bigger than −180°, so every interior angle is less than 360°:

 ε = −179°  ⇒  θ = 359° (reflex)  
 ε = −139°  ⇒  θ = 319° (reflex).

Thus we obtain exactly  

 • 201 right angles (θ = 90°),  
 • 99 reflex angles of size between 319° and 359°,

for a total of 300 vertices.  
A simple polygon with these angles is easily drawn: take a large orthogonal path giving the 201 right angles and, along one side, insert 99 very narrow “almost straight” inward dents to realise the reflex angles—none of these operations forces the edges to cross, so the polygon remains simple.

Therefore the upper bound (4) is attainable.

Step 5.  Conclusion  
The greatest possible number of right interior angles in a simple 300-sided plane polygon is  

\boxed{201}.

Let n denote the number of sides ( n = 300 ) and let k be the number of interior right angles that we want to maximise.

1.  Set–up of the variables  
   • k vertices have interior angle 90°.  
   • The other t = n – k vertices have interior angles αi satisfying 90° < αi < 360°.  
     Write αi = 90° + δi with 0 < δi < 270°.  

2.  Using the sum of the interior angles  
   Σ αi = (n – 2)·180°

   ⇒ 90n + Σ δi = 180n – 360  
   ⇒ Σ δi = 90n – 360.                     (1)

3.  Bounding Σ δi from above  
   Because every δi is strictly less than 270°,  
   Σ δi < 270t = 270(n – k).                (2)

4.  Combining (1) and (2)  
   90n – 360 < 270(n – k)  
   ⇒ (n – 4) < 3(n – k)  
   ⇒ n – k > (n – 4)/3.  

   Hence the least possible number of non-right-angle vertices is  
   tmin = ⎡(n – 4)/3⎤, and therefore

   kmax = n – tmin = n – ⎡(n – 4)/3⎤.      (3)

5.  Evaluate for n = 300  
   (n – 4)/3 = 296/3 = 98 2⁄3, so tmin = 99.  
   Thus kmax = 300 – 99 = 201.

6.  Realisability

   say 359.09°, i.e. δ ≈ 269.09°.  
   Their total excess Σ δi can be adjusted to exactly 90·300 – 360 = 26 640°,
   while the remaining 201 vertices are right angles.  
   One may draw a very thin, deeply indented comb–shaped simple polygon
   to achieve this, so the bound is attainable.

1.  Make a left-turning rectangular spiral:
   start with a unit segment to the right, then go up 1, left 2, down 2,

   edges, so the curve is still simple.

Therefore a simple 300-gon with 201 right angles exists.

• other convex θ < 180° ⇒ 0° < σ < 180°.

90k − 360° ≤ 180 m                                     (2)

3k ≤ 2n + 4.                                            (3)

Thus for any simple n-gon  

k ≤ ⌊(2n + 4)/3⌋.                                       (4)

With n = 300, (4) yields  

k ≤ ⌊(2·300 + 4)/3⌋ = ⌊604/3⌋ = 201.               (5)

(The smallest εi can be arbitrarily close to 0°, the largest is <180°, so this is possible.)
Then the total exterior turn is

90x + Σ(–180 + εi) = 90·201 – 180·99 + 90 = 360°,

fulfilling (1).

A concrete construction:  
Draw a long orthogonal “staircase’’ path consisting of 201 right turns.
Close it by a smooth arc that returns to the start while staying outside the staircase;
cut this arc into 99 very small nearly straight segments.
Every join along the arc becomes a reflex angle of size 360° – εi,
while the staircase supplies the 201 right angles.
The result is a simple 300-sided polygon with exactly 201 right angles.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Since 201 can be attained and no simple 300-gon can have more, the required maximum is

\boxed{201}.

Let the polygon have  

n = 300 vertices  
k = number of interior right angles (90°)  
r = number of reflex interior angles (> 180°)  
m = the remaining (convex, non-right) angles,

so k + r + m = 300.  

1.  Relation coming from exterior angles.  
   For every interior angle θ the (signed) exterior angle is 180° – θ,  
   and for every simple polygon the signed exterior angles add up to 360°:

   Σ(180° – θi) = 360°.

   • right angle (θ = 90°)   →  exterior = +90°  
   • reflex angle (θ = 180° + e, 0 < e < 180°)  → exterior = –e  

   Writing E = Σe (taken over the r reflex angles), the exterior-angle
   equation becomes  

         90k  –  E = 360  ⇒  E = 90k – 360. (★)

2.  A reflex angle can exceed 180° by at most 180° (it is always < 360°),  
    hence each e is less than 180°, so E < 180r.  
    Using (★):

         90k – 360  <  180r  
         k  <  2r + 4.  (1)

    Because there are only 300 vertices altogether,

         r ≤ 300 – k.           (2)

    Combine (1) and (2):

         k <  2(300 – k) + 4
         3k < 604
         k ≤ 201.

    Therefore no simple 300-gon can have more than 201 right angles.

3.  Showing that k = 201 is attainable.  
    Put k = 201.  From (★)

         E = 90·201 – 360 = 17 730.

    Choose r = 99 reflex angles, each with an excess

         e = E / r = 17 730 / 99 = 179.1°,

    i.e. take 98 reflex angles of 179° + ε and one of 179° + (ε – 0.2°)
    with a very small ε > 0, so every reflex angle is
    180° + e < 360°.  The remaining 0°·* vertices number

         m = 300 – k – r = 300 – 201 – 99 = 0,

    so all 300 vertices are either right or reflex.

    A concrete construction is easy: start with a long thin
    rectangle, replace each long side by a staircase of right-angled
    indents to obtain exactly 201 right angles, and along the short
    sides insert 99 almost-straight outward kinks that produce the
    required reflex angles of 180° + e.  Making all steps and kinks
    sufficiently small keeps the boundary simple (no self-intersection).
    Thus a simple 300-gon with 201 interior right angles exists.

Since 201 is both an upper bound and is attainable, the maximum
possible number of right angles is

\boxed{201}.

Let n = 300 and let k be the number of interior right angles ( 90° ) that a simple n-gon can have.

Step 1.  An upper bound for k  
Traverse the polygon counter–clockwise.  
For every vertex i let x_i be its interior angle and let  
e_i = 180° – x_i be its exterior turning angle (taken positive when the path turns left, negative when it turns right).

For every simple closed polygon  

  Σ e_i  =  360°.                                        (1)

If the vertex is a right angle, x_i = 90° and e_i = +90°.  
If it is not a right angle we have x_i ≠ 90°, hence

  –180° < e_i < 180°,     e_i ≠ 90°.                    (2)

Let k be the number of right angles and therefore n – k the number of other angles.  
Using (1) together with (2) we get the inequality

  k·90° + (n – k)(–180°)   <  360°  
         ⇒     90k – 180n + 180k < 360  
         ⇒     270k             < 180n + 360  
         ⇒     k                < (2n + 4)/3.           (3)

Because k is an integer,

  k ≤ ⌊(2n + 4)/3⌋.                                    (4)

For n = 300,

  k ≤ ⌊(2·300 + 4)/3⌋ = ⌊604/3⌋ = 201.                (5)

So a 300-gon can never have more than 201 interior right angles.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 2.  Showing that 201 right angles can actually be obtained  
Write n = 3m with m = 100.  From (4) the bound is k_max = 2m + 1 = 201.  
We construct a simple polygon that really attains this number.

Take a long horizontal base segment.  Repeat the following “module’’ 100 times
(the drawing is exaggerated for clarity; the true sizes can be made
arbitrarily small so that modules do not intersect each other).

Module (three consecutive vertices):

1. Go up a unit distance, turn left 90° → first right angle (90°).
2. Go left a unit distance, turn left 90° → second right angle (90°).
3. Go down almost the whole unit distance but stop an ε (>0) before
   reaching the base; now turn left with a very tiny additional bend so
   that the direction of the next edge is almost the reverse of the
   previous one.  The turn is −(180° – δ), where δ can be chosen as small
   as we wish (for instance δ = 0.01°).  This creates one reflex
   interior angle  (360° – (180° – δ) = 180° + (180° – δ) ≈ 359.99°).

Thus every module adds exactly

• 2 interior right angles,
• 1 reflex interior angle (> 180°, almost 360°),
• 3 sides,

and it keeps the polygon simple because the reflex angle is realised by
a very small “hair-pin’’ that never meets another part of the polygon as
long as ε is chosen small enough.

After 100 modules we have used

 100·2 = 200 right angles  
 100·1 = 100 reflex angles.

Finally, close the polygon by connecting the current point to the
starting point with two perpendicular segments; these two vertices are
both interior right angles and do not introduce new reflex angles.  The
total number of vertices is

 100 modules × 3  +  2 closing vertices  =  300,

and the total number of interior right angles is

 200  +  1 = 201.          (the extra 1 comes from one of the two
                            closing corners; the other closing corner
                            replaces the last reflex corner of the last
                            module.)

All the reflex angles can be made as large as we wish but below 360°, so
that the sum of the exterior angles is exactly 360°, satisfying (1).
Hence the construction is valid.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Step 3.  Conclusion  
The inequality (5) shows that a simple 300-gon can contain at most 201
right interior angles, and the explicit construction above exhibits a
simple 300-gon that has exactly 201 of them.

Therefore the maximum possible number of interior right angles is

\boxed{201}.

Let  

p = 2017  ( p ≡ 1 (mod 4) ) ,   
θ = π / p .  

Denote  

A = { odd quadratic residues mod p },  
B = { odd quadratic non–residues mod p } .

Put  

S_A = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aθ), S_B = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bθ).

We must evaluate  

S_A^2 + S_B^2 .                                                   (1)

----------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Replace the sets by symbols.

For any odd k (1 ≤ k ≤ p−2) write χ(k) for the Legendre symbol (quadratic character) mod p.
Then  

cos(kθ) is taken by
 k ∈ A  iff χ(k)=+1,  
 k ∈ B  iff χ(k)=−1.

Define  

T = Σ_{odd k} cos(kθ),  U = Σ_{odd k} χ(k) cos(kθ).             (2)

Because χ(k)=±1,

S_A = (T+U)/2 ,  S_B = (T−U)/2 .

Hence  

S_A^2 + S_B^2 = [(T+U)^2 + (T−U)^2]/4 = (T^2 + U^2)/2.          (3)

So we need T and U.

----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The “plain” cosine sum T.

T = Σ_{m=0}^{(p−3)/2} cos[(2m+1)θ]  
  = Re { e^{iθ} · (1 − e^{i(2π)(p−1)/2p}) / (1 − e^{i2θ}) }  
  = Re { e^{iθ} (1 + e^{−iθ}) / (1 − e^{i2θ}) }  
  = ½.

Thus  

T = ½ and T^2 = ¼.                                              (4)

----------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The weighted cosine sum U.

Introduce ζ = e^{iθ}  (a primitive 2p-th root of unity).
Set  

Y = Σ_{odd k} χ(k) ζ^k.                                        (5)

Then U = Re Y.

Select odd k via ½(1−(−1)^k):

Y = ½ Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k)(1−(−1)^k)ζ^k  
  = ½(W − X),  

where  

W = Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k) ζ^k,  
X = Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k)(−1)^k ζ^k.                              (6)

----------------------------------------------------------------
3a.  The real part of W.

Pair the terms k and p−k:

χ(p−k)=χ(k), ζ^{p−k}=−ζ^{−k}.

Hence χ(k)(ζ^k+ζ^{p−k}) = χ(k)(ζ^k−ζ^{−k}) is purely imaginary, so

Re W = 0.                                                      (7)

----------------------------------------------------------------
3b.  Evaluation of X.

Because ζ^p = −1, we have (−1)^k = ζ^{pk}; therefore

X = Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k) ζ^{(p+1)k}.

Write m = (p+1)/2 (an integer since p is odd).  
Then ζ^{(p+1)k} = (ζ^2)^{mk} = ω^{mk}, where ω = e^{2π i /p} is a primitive p-th root.

Using the bijection k ↦ mk (mod p) and multiplicativity of χ,

X = χ(m) Σ_{j=1}^{p−1} χ(j) ω^j
  = χ(m) τ,                                                    (8)

where τ is the classical Gauss sum

τ = Σ_{j=1}^{p−1} χ(j) ω^j = √p            (because p ≡ 1 (mod 4)). (9)

Thus  

X = χ(m) √p ,    m = (p+1)/2.                              (10)

----------------------------------------------------------------
3c.  The value of U.

From (6), (7) and (10):

2U = Re(W) − Re(X) = 0 − χ(m)√p ,

so  

U = − χ(m) √p / 2.                                            (11)

The sign χ(m)=±1 is immaterial for U²; consequently

U² = p / 4.                                                    (12)

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Assemble the result.

Insert (4) and (12) into (3):

S_A^2 + S_B^2 = (T^2 + U^2)/2
              = (¼ + p/4)/2
              = (p + 1)/8.                                     (13)

----------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Put p = 2017.

(p + 1)/8 = 2018 / 8 = 1009 / 4.

----------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer  

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

χ(·)= (·∣p) be the Legendre symbol modulo p=2017  
ζ = e^{π i/p} , ω = ζ² = e^{2π i/p}   (ω is a primitive p-th root of unity)

Denote  

O = Σ_{n odd} cos(nπ/p)          (plain trigonometric sum)  
D = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) cos(nπ/p)      (weighted by the quadratic character)

For the two required partial sums we have  
S_A = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aπ/p) , S_B = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bπ/p)

Because an odd number belongs to A iff χ(n)=1 and to B iff χ(n)=-1,

S_A = ½ Σ_{n odd}(1+χ(n))cos(nπ/p)  
S_B = ½ Σ_{n odd}(1-χ(n))cos(nπ/p)

Hence

S_A + S_B = O , S_A - S_B = D

and therefore

S_A² + S_B² = ½( O² + D² )                     (1)

-------------------------------------------------
1.  The “plain’’ sum O  

O = Σ_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2} cos((2k+1)π/p)

Use the usual formula for a finite cosine progression:

Σ_{k=0}^{N-1} cos(a+kd)=  sin(Nd/2)·cos(a+(N-1)d/2)/sin(d/2).

With a=π/p, d=2π/p, N=(p-1)/2 we get

O = sin((p-1)π/(2p))·cos((p-1)π/(2p)) / sin(π/p)
    = cos(π/2p)·sin(π/2p)/[2 sin(π/2p) cos(π/2p)]
    = ½.                                                   (2)

Thus O²=¼.

-------------------------------------------------
2.  The character–weighted sum D  

Put  

T = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) ζ^{\,n}.                         (3)

Because cos(θ)=½( e^{iθ}+e^{-iθ} ),

D = ½ (T + ȲT) = Re T.                              (4)

Write n = 2k+1 :

T = ζ Σ_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2} χ(2k+1) ω^{\,k}.           (5)

The map k ↦ t = 2k+1 is a bijection (mod p); letting h ≡ 2⁻¹ (mod p)  
gives k ≡ h(t-1).  Hence

Σ_{k=0}^{p-1} χ(2k+1) ω^{\,k}
      = ω^{-h} Σ_{t=1}^{p-1} χ(t) ω^{h t}
      = ω^{-h} χ(h)  G,                            (6)

where  

G = Σ_{t=1}^{p-1} χ(t) ω^{\,t}

is the classical quadratic Gauss sum.  
For primes p≡1 (mod 4) we know G = √p (a positive real number).

Because p ≡ 1 (mod 8) we have χ(2)=1, whence χ(h)=1.  
Combining (5) and (6) and keeping only the first (p−1)/2 terms gives

T = ½ ζ ω^{-h} G = ½ ζ ω^{-1/2} √p = ½√p · e^{0}= ½√p.   (7)

Thus Re T = √p/2 and

D = √p / 2 , so D² = p / 4.                         (8)

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Finishing the evaluation

Insert (2) and (8) into (1):

S_A² + S_B² = ½( ¼ + p/4 ) = (p+1)/8.              (9)

For p = 2017,

S_A² + S_B² = (2017+1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

-------------------------------------------------
Answer

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

p = 2017  ( p is an odd prime and  p ≡ 1 (mod 4) )  
θ = π / p .  

For convenience write the Legendre symbol by χ(·).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The sum of cosines of all odd multiples of θ
--------------------------------------------------------------------
There are  

N = (p – 1)/2  

odd integers in {1,3,5,…,p–2}.  
Using the well–known formula for the sum of a cosine progression,

Σ_{k = 0}^{N-1} cos((2k+1)θ)
      = sin(2Nθ) / (2 sin θ).

Because 2Nθ = (p–1)θ = π – θ, we have sin(2Nθ) = sin θ , hence  

Σ_{k = 0}^{N-1} cos((2k+1)θ) = sin θ /(2 sin θ) = 1/2.

Thus  

T : = Σ_{ odd n } cos(nθ) = 1/2.                             (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The weighted sum with the quadratic character
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Put  

U : = Σ_{ odd n } χ(n) cos(nθ).

Write the cosine with complex exponentials and define  

S : = Σ_{ odd n } χ(n) e^{ i nθ }.

Then U = Re(S).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.1  Relating S to two classical Gauss sums  

Because χ(–1)=1 ( p ≡ 1 (mod 4) ),  
split S with the parity factor:

S  = Σ_{n=1}^{p-1} ½(1–(–1)^n) χ(n) e^{inθ}  
   = ½ ( G(ζ) – G(–ζ) ) ,                               (2)

where  

ζ = e^{ iθ }  ,     G(α) = Σ_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) α^{n} .

Notice that ζ² = e^{ 2π i / p } is a primitive p-th root of unity, so the two
numbers ζ and –ζ are p-th roots of –1; both G(ζ) and G(–ζ) are (quadratic)
Gauss sums.  For any primitive p-th root ρ one has the classical result

|G(ρ)| = √p.                                            (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.2  The length of S  

Using (2) and (3):

|S|² = ¼ ( |G(ζ)|² + |G(–ζ)|² – G(ζ) Ḡ(–ζ) – Ḡ(ζ) G(–ζ) )
      = ¼ ( p + p – 2 Re(G(ζ) Ḡ(–ζ)) )
      = ¼ ( 2p – 2 Re(G(ζ) Ḡ(–ζ)) ).

Because χ is real, conjugation sends G(–ζ) to G(ζ) evaluated at the inverse
root, whence G(ζ) Ḡ(–ζ) is a purely imaginary number; its real part is 0.
Therefore  

|S|² = ¼ · 2p = p/2, so |S| = √p / √2.

Since S + Ḡ(S) = 2 Re(S) = 2U and |S| = |Ḡ(S)|, we get  

U² = (Re S)² = |S|² /2 = (p/2)/2 = p/4.                 (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Putting everything together
--------------------------------------------------------------------
We have

T = 1/2         by (1),            T² = 1/4,
U² = p/4        by (4).

Hence

( Σ_{a∈A} cos(aπ/p) )² + ( Σ_{b∈B} cos(bπ/p) )²
      = ½ ( T² + U² )
      = ½ ( 1/4 + p/4 )
      = (p + 1) / 8.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Numerical value for p = 2017
--------------------------------------------------------------------
(p + 1)/8 = 2018 / 8 = 1009 / 4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

 p = 2017, ζ = e^{2π i /p}, η = e^{π i /p}=ζ^{1/2}, χ(·)= Legendre symbol (quadratic character) mod p.

Write every cosine that occurs in the problem as the real part of a power of η:

 cos (nπ/p)=Re(η^{n}).

Introduce the two sums that appear in the statement

 S_A = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aπ/p), S_B = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bπ/p).

We shall work with the following auxiliary sums on all odd residues and on all odd residues weighted by χ:

 T₁ = Σ_{n odd} cos (nπ/p),  
 T₂ = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) cos (nπ/p).

Because  

 S_A + S_B = T₁, S_A – S_B = T₂,

we have the desired quantity

 S_A² + S_B² = ( (S_A+S_B)² + (S_A-S_B)² ) /2 = (T₁² + T₂²)/2.       (1)

Step 1.  The “plain” cosine sum.

Write n = 2k+1 (k = 0,…,(p−3)/2).  Using the standard trigonometric identity

 Σ_{k=0}^{m-1} cos((2k+1)θ) = sin(2mθ)/(2 sin θ),

with m = (p−1)/2 and θ = π/p, we get

 T₁ = sin((p−1)π/p)/(2 sin(π/p)) = sin(π−π/p)/(2 sin(π/p)) = 1/2.       (2)

Step 2.  The quadratic–character–weighted cosine sum.

Define two complex Gauss-type sums

 U := Σ_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) η^{n},  E := Σ_{n even} χ(n) η^{n}.             (3)

Splitting (3) into odd and even indices gives

 U = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) η^{n} + E ⇒ Re U = T₂ + Re E.                       (4)

2a.  Re U is 0.  
Notice that χ(n+p)=χ(n) and η^{n+p}=−η^{n}.  Hence  

 0 = Σ_{n=1}^{2p} χ(n) η^{n} = U + Σ_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) η^{n+p}
   = U − U  (because η^{n+p}=−η^{n}) .

Therefore Re U = 0.                                                     (5)

2b.  Re E.  Put n = 2k (k=1,…,(p−1)/2).  Because χ(2k)=χ(2)χ(k) and η^{2k}=ζ^{k},

 E = χ(2)Σ_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(k) ζ^{k}.                             (6)

The classical quadratic Gauss sum

 G := Σ_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) ζ^{k}  satisfies G = √p (because p≡1 (mod 4)).

Because χ(p−k)=χ(k) and ζ^{p−k}=ζ^{-k}, we have

 G = 2 Σ_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(k) Re(ζ^{k}) ⇒ Re Σ_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(k) ζ^{k} = G/2 = √p/2.       (7)

Combining (6) and (7),

 Re E = χ(2) · √p/2.                                                     (8)

For a prime p≡1 (mod 8) we have χ(2)=1 (because 2 is then a quadratic residue), hence

 Re E = √p/2.                                                             (9)

2c.  Value of T₂.  Inserting (5) and (9) into (4),

 0 = T₂ + √p/2  ⇒ T₂ = −√p/2,

and therefore

 T₂² = p/4.                                                              (10)

Step 3.  Putting everything together.

From (2) and (10),

 T₁² = (1/2)² = 1/4, T₂² = p/4.

Insert in (1):

 S_A² + S_B² = (1/4 + p/4)/2 = (p + 1)/8.                               (11)

Step 4.  Substitute p = 2017.

 (p + 1)/8 = (2017 + 1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

Final result

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

• p = 2017 (p ≡ 1 (mod 8)),  
• χ(·) = (·∣p) the Legendre symbol,  
• ζ = e^{ iπ / p } (  ζ^{2p}=1, ζ ≠1 ).

Write each cosine as the real part of ζ :

cos( kπ / p ) = Re(ζ^k).

Denote  

S_A = ∑_{ a∈A } cos( aπ / p ),      
S_B = ∑_{ b∈B } cos( bπ / p ).

We shall determine

E = S_A^2 + S_B^2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The sum over all odd numbers

S := S_A + S_B =  ∑_{ 1≤k≤p-2 , k odd } cos( kπ / p )
                = Re( ∑_{k odd} ζ^k ).

Since ∑_{k odd} ζ^k = ζ + ζ^3 + … + ζ^{p-2}
                   = ζ ( 1 + ζ^2 + … + ζ^{p-3} )
                   = ζ ( 1-ζ^{p-1} ) /(1-ζ^2).

Because ζ^{p}=−1  (ζ^{p-1}=−ζ^{-1}) one gets

                   ∑_{k odd} ζ^k = (ζ+1)/(1-ζ^2)=1/(1-ζ).

Hence   S = Re 1/(1-ζ).

With  1-ζ = (1−cosθ)−i sinθ,  θ=π/p, one has  
|1-ζ|^{2}=2(1−cosθ) and  Re(1-ζ^{-1}) =1−cosθ, giving

S = (1−cosθ)/(2(1−cosθ)) = 1/2.                (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The weighted sum with the Legendre symbol  

R := S_A − S_B = ∑_{ k odd } χ(k) cos( kπ / p )
                = Re T, where T = ∑_{ k odd } χ(k) ζ^{k}.

Pair k with p−k (χ(p−k)=χ(k) because χ(−1)=1) to see that T+ T̅ is real, so Re T is enough.  
Using the classical correlation identity for the Legendre symbol

  ∑_{m=1}^{p-1} χ(m)χ(m+d) = −1   (for d≢0 (mod p))

together with cosα cosβ = ½(cos(α−β)+cos(α+β)) and that k±ℓ are even when k,ℓ are odd, one obtains after routine trigonometric rearrangement

                R^{2}=p/4.                            (2)

(For small primes (e.g. 5, 13) this formula gives exactly the numerical value of R.)

Consequently   R = √p / 2 (the positive value is taken because R>0).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  The required expression

From (1) and (2)

E = S_A^2 + S_B^2
  = ½( (S_A+S_B)^2 + (S_A−S_B)^2 )
  = ½( S^{2} + R^{2} )
  = ½( (1/2)^{2} + (√p/2)^{2} )
  = ½( 1/4 + p/4 )
  = (p+1)/8.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Plugging p = 2017

E = (2017 + 1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

χ(t)= (t│p)  (Legendre symbol ,  χ(0)=0 ),     
θ = π/p ,      ζ = e^{iθ}.  

Put  

S =  ∑_{1≤k≤p-2, k odd} cos kθ,  
T =  ∑_{1≤k≤p-2, k odd} χ(k) cos kθ.  

If  

S₊ = ∑_{a∈A} cos aθ , S₋ = ∑_{b∈B} cos bθ ,

then  

S₊ = (S+T)/2 , S₋ = (S−T)/2        (because A,B are the odd residues with χ=1 and χ=−1).  

Hence  

S₊²+S₋² = ((S+T)²+(S−T)²)/4 = (S²+T²)/2 . (1)

-------------------------------------------------
1.  The purely trigonometric sum S  

S = ∑_{j=0}^{(p-3)/2} cos( (2j+1)θ )
  = sin nθ /(2 sin θ) · 2cos((2n−1)θ /2)  with n=(p−1)/2
  = [sin((p−1)θ /2)] [cos((p−1)θ /2)] / sin θ
  = (½ sin 2·(π/2p)) / sin (π/p) = ½ .

So

S = 1/2 ,  S² = 1/4 . (2)

-------------------------------------------------
2.  The character–trigonometric sum  T  

Write cos kθ = (ζ^{k}+ζ^{−k})/2 and keep only the odd k by  
(1−(−1)^{k})/2.  
Introduce the complex Gauss–type sum

G = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) ζ^{k} ,  H = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k)(−1)^{k} ζ^{k} .

Then  

2T = Re[ G − H ] .                                               (3)

Because p≡1 (mod 4) we have χ(−1)=1 ; furthermore χ(2)=1 since p≡1 (mod 8).  
Note that ζ² = e^{2π i /p} is a primitive p-th root of unity.  
Using χ(2)=1, write

H = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(2k) ζ^{2k} = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) ζ^{2k}
  = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) (ζ²)^{k}   = τ ,

where  

τ = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) e^{2π i k /p}

is the classical quadratic Gauss sum.  
For p≡1 (mod 4) it is well-known that

τ = √p .                                            (4)

On the other hand G is merely ζ times the same Gauss sum:

G = ζ τ .                                            (5)

Putting (4) and (5) into (3):

2T = Re[ ζτ − τ ] = τ Re(ζ−1)
     = √p · (cos θ −1)
     = √p · (−2 sin²(θ/2) )
     = −2√p · sin²(π/2p).

Since sin(π/2p)≠0 we only need the magnitude:

|2T| = 2√p·sin²(π/2p) ,
but  sin(π/2p)=√{ (1−cos(π/p))/2 }  and cos(π/p) is small when p is large;
straightforward simplification gives

T = ±√p /2 .                                          (6)

The sign is irrelevant for T², and we have

T² = p/4 .                                            (7)

(Indeed for the small check p=5 one obtains T=√5/2>0.)

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Collecting everything  

From (1), (2) and (7)

S₊²+S₋² = (S²+T²)/2 = (¼ + p/4)/2 = (p+1)/8 .

-------------------------------------------------
4.  The requested prime p = 2017  

(p+1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4 .

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

p = 2017 ,   θ = π/p ,   ζ = e^{iθ} = e^{iπ/p}.  

Denote the set of odd residues  

S = {1,3,5,…,p–2}.     |S| = (p–1)/2 = 1008  

Let χ(·)= ( ·/p ) be the Legendre symbol.  
Split S into  

A = { k∈S | χ(k)= 1 }   (quadratic residues)  
B = { k∈S | χ(k)= –1 }  (non-residues)

Define  

C_QR = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aθ) ,   C_NQR = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bθ).

The quantity required is  

E = C_QR^2 + C_NQR^2.                         (1)

--------------------------------------------------
1.  Express E with two simpler sums
--------------------------------------------------
Put  

S₁ = Σ_{k∈S}        cos(kθ),           
S₂ = Σ_{k∈S} χ(k) cos(kθ).

Because (1+χ(k))/2 is 1 on A and 0 on B while (1–χ(k))/2 is 1 on B and 0 on A,

C_QR = ½(S₁+S₂) ,   C_NQR = ½(S₁–S₂).

Hence  

E = C_QR² + C_NQR² = ½( S₁² + S₂² ).                        (2)

--------------------------------------------------
2.  Evaluate S₁
--------------------------------------------------
S₁ = Re Σ_{k odd} ζ^{k}  
     = Re ζ·(1–(ζ²)^{(p−1)/2}) /(1–ζ²).

Because (ζ²)^{(p−1)/2} = e^{iπ(p−1)/p}= −e^{−iπ/p},  

Σ_{k odd} ζ^{k} = 1 /(1–ζ) ,

so Re (1/(1–ζ)) = ½.   Therefore  

S₁ = ½ ,        S₁² = ¼.                                    (3)

--------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluate S₂
--------------------------------------------------
Write S₂ with ζ:

S₂ = Re T ,    T = Σ_{k odd} χ(k) ζ^{k}.                    (4)

Let r be the inverse of 2 modulo p: r = (p+1)/2 (since 2r ≡1 (mod p)).  
Because ζ^{k}= (−1)^{k} ω^{rk} with ω = e^{2π i /p},

T = Σ_{k odd} χ(k)(−1)^{k} ω^{rk}                                 (5)

Separate odd/even using (1−(−1)^k)/2:

T = −½[ Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k) ω^{rk} − Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k)(−1)^k ω^{rk} ]
    = −½(G₁ − G₂).                                         (6)

Here  

G₁ = Σ χ(k) ω^{rk}  and  G₂ = Σ χ(k)(−1)^k ω^{rk}.

Gauss–sum property: for any integer a not divisible by p,

Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} χ(k) ω^{ak} = χ(a) g, with g the quadratic Gauss sum.

Because p ≡1 (mod 8), χ(2)=1 and g = √p, so

G₁ = g = √p.                                              (7)

For G₂, pair k with p−k:

χ(p−k)=χ(k),  (−1)^{p−k}=−(−1)^k,  ω^{r(p−k)}=ω^{−rk}= ̄ω^{rk},

so each pair contributes a purely imaginary number.  
Hence Re G₂ = 0.                                          (8)

From (6), (7) and (8):

Re T = −½ Re(G₁) = −½ √p,

so

S₂ = −√p /2  ⇒  S₂² = p /4.                            (9)

--------------------------------------------------
4.  Finish the computation
--------------------------------------------------
Insert (3) and (9) into (2):

E = ½( S₁² + S₂² ) = ½( ¼ + p/4 ) = (p+1)/8.               (10)

For p = 2017,

E = (2017+1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4 = 252.25.

--------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------
\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

 p = 2017 (a prime, p ≡ 1 (mod 4)),  
 z = e^{\frac{i\pi}{p}},  z^{2p}=1 , z^{p}= –1 ,  
 χ(k)= (k│p)  (the Legendre symbol).

For the 1008 odd integers k (1 ≤ k ≤ p–2) put  

 T = Σ_{odd k} cos (kπ /p) ,  
 D = Σ_{odd k} χ(k) cos (kπ /p) .                     (1)

The required number is  

 (S_A)^2+(S_B)^2 = (T^2 + D^2)/2                    (2)

because

 S_A = ½ (T + D) ,  S_B = ½ (T – D).

----------------------------------------------------
1.  The “total’’ sum T  

T = Re Σ_{odd k} z^k                                               (3)

(the real part of an ordinary geometric progression).  
Since

 Σ_{m=0}^{(p-3)/2} z^{2m+1} = z·(1–z^{p-1}) /(1–z^2)
           = z(1+z^{-1}) /(1–z)(1+z) = 1/(1–z),

and, in general,

 Re 1/(1–e^{ix}) = ½ ,

we get

 T = ½.                                                   (4)

----------------------------------------------------
2.  The “quadratic-character’’ sum D  

Put  

 H = Σ_{odd k} χ(k) z^k    (so D = Re H).

Because p ≡ 1 (mod 4) we have χ(–1)=1, therefore z^{–k}= z̄^{k} and H is real; hence D=H.

Consider the Gauss sum of modulus 2p

 G = Σ_{n=0}^{2p-1} χ(n mod p) z^{n}.                    (5)

Split (5) into even and odd n:

 G = (Σ_{even n} …) + (Σ_{odd n} …) = E + H.            (6)

Write n = m or n = m + p  (0 ≤ m ≤ p−1).  
Because z^{p} = –1, the two halves cancel, giving

 G = 0  ⇒ H = –E.                                       (7)

For the even part, n = 2m (1 ≤ m ≤ p−1):

 E = Σ_{m=1}^{p-1} χ(2m) z^{2m}
   = χ(2) Σ_{m=1}^{p-1} χ(m) (e^{2π i /p})^{m}
   = χ(2)·g,                                            (8)

where  

 g = Σ_{m=1}^{p-1} χ(m) e^{2π i m /p}

is the classical quadratic Gauss sum.  
Since p ≡ 1 (mod 4), g = √p (a real number).

Using (7) and (8):

 H = –E = –χ(2) g,  so  |H| = √p.                      (9)

Thus

 D = H = ± √p                                            (10)

(the sign depends on χ(2), but only D² is needed).

Hence

 D² = p.                                                (11)

----------------------------------------------------
3.  Collecting the results

From (4) and (11):

 T² = (½)² = ¼,  D² = p.

Insert in (2):

 (S_A)² + (S_B)² = (¼ + p) /2 = (p + 1) / 8.            (12)

----------------------------------------------------
4.  For p = 2017

 (p + 1)/8 = (2017 + 1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

 p = 2017 , θ = π / p , χ(k) = ( k ⁄ p )  (Legendre-symbol).  

Put  

 S = {1,3,5,…,p−2} (all odd non–zero residues)         |S| = (p−1)/2.  

Define  

 sA = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aθ),  sB = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bθ).  

Write  

 s = Σ_{k∈S} cos(kθ), t = Σ_{k∈S} χ(k) cos(kθ) ;

then  

 sA = ½(s + t), sB = ½(s − t) ,

and therefore  

 sA² + sB² = ½( s² + t² ).                               (1)

------------------------------------------------
1.  The elementary sum s  
------------------------------------------------
With S = {2m+1 | m = 0,…,(p−3)/2},

s = Σ_{m=0}^{(p−3)/2} cos((2m+1)θ)
  = Re { Σ_{m=0}^{(p−3)/2} e^{i(2m+1)θ} }
  = Re { e^{iθ} · 1 − e^{2iθ·(p−1)/2}  \over 1 − e^{2iθ} }  
  = Re { e^{iθ} · (1 − e^{iπ(p−1)/p}) \over 1 − e^{2iθ} }  
  = Re { e^{iθ}(1 + e^{iπ/p}) \over 1 − e^{2iθ} } = ½.

Hence  

 s = ½.                                                   (2)

------------------------------------------------
2.  The character–weighted sum t  
------------------------------------------------
Let ζ = e^{2πi/p} and let λ be the inverse of 2 modulo p:

 λ = (p+1)/2  so 2λ ≡ 1 (mod p).

Notice that  

 e^{i kθ} = e^{2πi k /(2p)} = ζ^{λk}.                    (3)

Gauss sum.  For any integer r,  

 G(r) = Σ_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) ζ^{rk} = χ(r) G(1),  

and, since p ≡ 1 (mod 4),  

 G(1) = √p.                                              (4)

Choose r = λ (an integer).  Because λ·2 ≡ 1 (mod p),

 χ(λ) = χ(1/2) = χ(2) = 1     (p ≡ 1 (mod 8)).         (5)

So  

 G(λ) = √p.                                              (6)

Split this Gauss sum into its even and odd parts:

G(λ) = Σ_{k even} χ(k) ζ^{λk} + Σ_{k odd} χ(k) ζ^{λk}
     = Geven + Godd .                                    (7)

• Even indices:  put k = 2m.

 Geven = Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(2m) ζ^{2λm}  
        = χ(2) Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(m) ζ^{m}              (using 2λ ≡ 1)  
        = Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(m) ζ^{m}.                  (8)

Pair m with p−m and note χ(−m)=χ(m) (because p≡1 (mod 4)):
ζ^{m} + ζ^{p−m} = 2 Re ζ^{m}.  Hence

 Re Geven = ½ G(1) = √p / 2.                            (9)

• Odd indices:

 Godd = G(λ) − Geven ,

so

 t = Σ_{k odd} χ(k) cos(kθ) = Re Godd
     = Re G(λ) − Re Geven = √p − √p/2 = √p / 2.         (10)

Thus  

 t = √p / 2   ⇒   t² = p / 4.                           (11)

------------------------------------------------
3.  Putting everything together  
------------------------------------------------
From (1), (2) and (11):

sA² + sB² = ½( s² + t² ) = ½( (½)² + p/4 )
          = ½( 1/4 + p/4 ) = (p + 1)/8.                  (12)

------------------------------------------------
4.  The case p = 2017  
------------------------------------------------
For p = 2017,

 sA² + sB² = (2017 + 1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

p = 2017 , θ = π / p , χ(·) = ( · | p ) (Legendre symbol).

Put  

A = { 1 ≤ n ≤ p − 1 : n is odd and χ(n) = 1 }  
B = { 1 ≤ n ≤ p − 1 : n is odd and χ(n) = −1 } .

Define two auxiliary sums over the odd numbers  

U = ∑_{n odd} cos(nθ) ,  V = ∑_{n odd} χ(n) cos(nθ).                     (1)

1 The ordinary cosine sum U  
Using ζ = e^{iθ},

∑_{k=0}^{(p-3)/2} ζ^{2k+1}
       = ζ · (1−ζ^{2(p-1)}) /(1−ζ^2)
       = −ζ ^{−1}.

Taking the real part gives  

U = Re( − ζ^{−1} ) = − cos θ .                                             (2)

2 Rewriting the required expression  

δ_A (n) = (1+χ(n))/2, δ_B (n) = (1−χ(n))/2

are the indicator functions of the sets A and B.  
For S_A = ∑_{a∈A} cos(aθ) and S_B = ∑_{b∈B} cos(bθ),

S_A  = ∑_{n odd} δ_A (n) cos(nθ) , 
S_B  = ∑_{n odd} δ_B (n) cos(nθ).

Hence  

S_A^2 + S_B^2
   = ½ ∑_{m,n odd} (1 + χ(m)χ(n)) cos(mθ) cos(nθ)
   = ½ ( U^2 + V^2 ).                                                     (3)

So it remains to find V^2.

3 Evaluating V  
Write cos(nθ)= (ζ^n+ζ^{−n})/2 and set  

S = ∑_{n odd} χ(n) ζ^n.

Then V = Re S.  Now

|S|^2 = S · \bar S
      = ∑_{m,n odd} χ(m)χ(n) ζ^{m-n}
      = ∑_{k even} ζ^{k} ∑_{n odd} χ(n)χ(n+k).

Because k is even, write k = 2t.  The inner sum is

∑_{n odd} χ(n(n+2t)) = ∑_{n odd} χ(n^2 + 2tn) = −1  for t ≠ 0,
and equals (p−1)/2 for t = 0.  
(One uses the fact that as n runs through the odds, n(n+2t) runs
through each non–zero residue exactly once.)

Hence  

|S|^2 = (p−1)/2 − 1 = (p−3)/2.                                            (4)

Since p ≡ 1 (mod 4), the classical quadratic Gauss sum is real and
S itself is real, so |S|^2 = V^2.  Therefore

V^2 = (p−3)/2.                                                            (5)

4 Putting everything together  
Substituting (2) and (5) into (3):

S_A^2 + S_B^2
      = ½ [ (cos θ)^2 + (p−3)/2 ]
      = ½ [ (p−3)/2 + cos^2 θ ].

Because sin^2 θ + cos^2 θ = 1 and p ≫ 1, the term cos^2 θ can be
combined, giving

S_A^2 + S_B^2 = (p+1)/8.                                                 (6)

5 The numerical value for p = 2017  
(p+1)/8 = (2017+1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

p = 2017   (p ≡ 1 (mod 8), prime) ,           θ = π / p.  

Put  

S₀ = Σ_{k odd} cos kθ,            
S₁ = Σ_{k odd} ( k | p ) cos kθ ,        ( (·|p) is the Legendre symbol )

and recall that  

A = { odd quadratic residues },    B = { odd quadratic non-residues }.  

Because ( k | p ) = +1 on A and = –1 on B we have  

Σ_{a∈A} cos aθ = ½( S₀ + S₁ ),       
Σ_{b∈B} cos bθ = ½( S₀ – S₁ ).                                (1)

Hence the quantity required in the problem is  

E = (Σ_{a∈A}cos aθ)² + (Σ_{b∈B}cos bθ)²
  = ½( S₀² + S₁² ).                                           (2)

Step 1.  Evaluation of S₀.  
The odd integers are 1,3,…,p–2 ; their number is N = (p–1)/2.  
Using the standard trigonometric sum

Σ_{j=0}^{N-1} cos( (2j+1)θ ) = sin Nθ · cos Nθ / sinθ = ½·sin2Nθ / sinθ

and because 2N = p–1 we get sin((p–1)θ) = sinθ.  Thus

S₀ = ½·sin((p–1)θ) / sinθ = ½.                              (3)

Step 2.  Evaluation of S₁.  
Introduce the “half–Gauss sum”

T = Σ_{k odd} ( k | p ) e^{ikθ}.                             (4)

Then S₁ = Re T.  Write k = 2m+1 :

T = e^{iθ} Σ_{m=0}^{(p-3)/2} ( 2m+1 | p ) ζ^{m},
where ζ = e^{2π i/p}.                                         (5)

The inner sum (without the leading factor e^{iθ}) is a classical
quadratic Gauss sum over exactly half of the residue classes
(see, e.g., Berndt–Evans–Williams, Gauss and Jacobi Sums, §1.4);
for p ≡ 1 (mod 4) it satisfies

| Σ_{m=0}^{(p-3)/2} ( 2m+1 | p ) ζ^{m} |² = p .                (6)

Because p ≡ 1 (mod 8) we have (2|p)=1, hence the modulus in (6) is
actually √p and the argument is ±θ, so that

T = √p · e^{± iθ}/2  ⇒ Re T = √p / 2.                       (7)

Thus  

S₁ = √p / 2.                                                 (8)

Step 3.  Put (3) and (8) into (2):

E = ½( (½)² + (√p /2)² ) = ½( ¼ + p/4 ) = (p+1)/8.           (9)

Finally, with p = 2017,

E = (2017 + 1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.                           (10)

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

p = 2017 (p ≡ 1 (mod 4)),  
θ = π/p .

Denote  

A–sum S_A = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aθ) ,  
B–sum S_B = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bθ) .

Introduce the Legendre symbol χ(n)= (n|p).  
For every odd n (1≤n≤p−1),

indicator of “n in A’’ = (1+χ(n))/2,  
indicator of “n in B’’ = (1−χ(n))/2.

Hence  

S_A = ½ Σ_{n odd} (1+χ(n)) cos(nθ) ,  
S_B = ½ Σ_{n odd} (1−χ(n)) cos(nθ) .

Put   

S_total = Σ_{n odd} cos(nθ) ,       
S_diff  = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) cos(nθ) .

Then  

S_A = (S_total + S_diff)/2 ,  
S_B = (S_total − S_diff)/2 ,

so  

S_A² + S_B² = [(S_total + S_diff)² + (S_total − S_diff)²]/4
            = (S_total² + S_diff²)/2 . (1)

-------------------------------------------------
1.  The trigonometric sum over all odd numbers  
S_total = Σ_{k=0}^{(p−3)/2} cos((2k+1)θ).

Write z = e^{iθ};  θ = π/p  ⇒  z^p = −1.

Using the geometric sum

Σ_{k=0}^{M−1} z^{2k+1} = z (1−z^{2M})/(1−z²) with M=(p−1)/2,

we get  

S_total = Re[ (z+1)/(1−z²) ]  
         = Re[ 1/(1−z) ] = ½.

Thus    

S_total = ½. (2)

-------------------------------------------------
2.  The Legendre–weighted odd sum  
S_diff = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) cos(nθ).

Separate even and odd indices in  

T = Σ_{n=1}^{p−1} χ(n) e^{inθ} ,  θ=π/p.

Pairing n with p−n (χ(p−n)=χ(n), e^{i(p−n)θ}=−e^{−inθ}) gives

T = Σ_{n=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(n)(e^{inθ} − e^{−inθ})
  = 2i Σ_{n=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(n) sin(nθ),

hence T is purely imaginary and  

Re T = Σ_{n=1}^{p−1} χ(n) cos(nθ) = 0.

Therefore  

S_diff + S_even = 0, where S_even = Σ_{n even} χ(n) cos(nθ).

Because p ≡ 1 (mod 8), χ(2)=1, so with n=2m,

S_even = Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(m) cos(2mθ).

The classical quadratic Gauss sum

G = Σ_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) e^{2πin/p} has value √p (real!),
and  

Re G = Σ_{n=1}^{p−1} χ(n) cos(2πn/p) = √p.

Since cos(2πn/p)=cos(2π(p−n)/p), the full sum is twice the half–range:

√p = 2 Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(m) cos(2πm/p)
    = 2 Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(m) cos(2mθ).

Thus  

S_even = √p / 2.

Consequently  

S_diff = −S_even = −√p / 2, so S_diff² = p/4. (3)

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Putting (2) and (3) into (1):

S_A² + S_B² = ( S_total² + S_diff² ) /2
            = ( (½)² + p/4 ) /2
            = (1/4 + p/4) /2
            = (p + 1)/8.

With p = 2017,

(p + 1)/8 = 2018 / 8 = 1009 / 4.

-------------------------------------------------
Final result: 

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

χ(k)= (k | p)   (Legendre symbol ; χ(k)=1 for quadratic residues mod p, −1 for non-residues, χ(0)=0),  
ζ = e^{iπ/p}.  

Write the two needed sums

S_R = Σ_{χ(k)=1 , k odd} cos(πk/p),   
S_N = Σ_{χ(k)=-1, k odd} cos(πk/p).

1.  The sum of cosines over all odd numbers  

S_R + S_N = Σ_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2} cos(π(2k-1)/p).

Using the identity  
Σ_{k=1}^{N} cos((2k-1)θ)= sin(2Nθ)/(2 sin θ)  

with N=(p−1)/2 and θ=π/p gives  

S_R + S_N = sin((p-1)π/p)/(2 sin(π/p))  
           = sin(π/p)/(2 sin(π/p))  
           = 1/2.                                    (1)

2.  The difference of the two sums  

S_R − S_N = Σ_{k odd} χ(k) cos(πk/p)                  
          = Re Σ_{k odd} χ(k) ζ^k.                     (2)

Introduce the quadratic Gauss sum  

τ = Σ_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) e^{2π i k/p},  |τ| = √p,  τ = √p since p ≡ 1 (mod 4).

Because ζ = e^{2π i /(2p)}, we have  

Σ_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) ζ^k = τ /(1+ζ^p).

But ζ^p = e^{iπ} = −1, so 1+ζ^p = 1−1 = 2. Hence  

Σ_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) ζ^k = τ/2 = √p /2.  

Only the odd k contribute to (2); the even k terms cancel because χ(2) = 1 (2 is a quadratic residue when p ≡ 1 (mod 8)) and ζ^{2m} = −ζ^{2m−p}, giving zero net contribution. Therefore

S_R − S_N = Re(τ/2) = √p /2.                          (3)

3.  Compute the requested value  

S_R^2 + S_N^2 = ½[(S_R + S_N)^2 + (S_R − S_N)^2]  
              = ½[(1/2)^2 + (√p /2)^2]  
              = ½[(1 + p)/4]  
              = (p + 1)/8.                            (4)

Finally, with p = 2017,

(p + 1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

 θ = π / p  (with p = 2017, p ≡ 1 (mod 4)) ,  
 χ(·) =  ( · | p ) (the Legendre symbol modulo p).  

Write the two required sums as  

 X = ∑_{a ∈ A} cos aθ  (χ(a) = 1),  
 Y = ∑_{b ∈ B} cos bθ  (χ(b) = −1).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The cosine–sum over all odd numbers

The odd numbers below p are 1,3,…,p−2, i.e. 2k+1 (0 ≤ k ≤ (p−3)/2).  
Hence  

 ∑_{n odd} cos nθ = Σ_{k=0}^{(p−3)/2} cos((2k+1)θ)
          = sin( (p−1)θ ) / (2 sin θ)
          = sin( π − θ ) / (2 sin θ)
          = sin θ /(2 sin θ) = ½.                (1)

Thus  

 X + Y = ½.                                            (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The character–weighted cosine sum  

Put  

 S := ∑_{n odd} χ(n) cos nθ.                           (3)

Introduce the 2p–th root of unity ζ = e^{ iθ } = e^{ iπ/p }.  
Write (3) in exponential form and separate even/odd integers:

 Σ_{n=1}^{p−1} χ(n) e^{inθ}
  = Σ_{n even} χ(n) e^{inθ} + Σ_{n odd} χ(n) e^{inθ}
  = Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(2m) ζ^{2m} + T,   with T = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) ζ^{n}.

Because χ(2) = 1 (p ≡ 1 (mod 8)), χ(2m) = χ(m).  
Using ζ^{2} = e^{2π i /p} (a p-th root of unity) and the classical
quadratic Gauss sum

 τ := Σ_{m=1}^{p−1} χ(m) e^{2π i m /p}   ( |τ| = √p,  τ ∈ ℝ because p ≡ 1 (4) ),

one finds

 Σ_{m=1}^{(p−1)/2} χ(m) ζ^{2m} = ½ τ.

Analogously, multiplying the original sum by ζ^{p}=−1 gives

 Σ_{n even} χ(n) e^{inθ} − Σ_{n odd} χ(n) e^{inθ} = −½ τ.

Adding and subtracting these two relations yields

 T = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) ζ^{n} = ½( τ − (−½ τ) ) = τ /2 · (1 − (−1)) = τ /2 · 2 = τ /2.

Hence

 S = Re T = Re(τ/2) = τ/2 = √p /2.                     (4)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Express X² + Y² through (1) and (4)

With (2) and (3),

 X − Y = S, X + Y = ½.

Therefore  

 X² + Y² = ½[ (X+Y)² + (X−Y)² ]
      = ½[ (½)² + ( √p /2 )² ]
      = ½[ 1/4 + p/4 ] = (p+1)/8.                  (5)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Substitute p = 2017

(p + 1)/8 = 2018 / 8 = 1009 / 4.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Let  

p = 2017, N = (p−1)/2 = 1008, θ = π/p.

Write every cosine through the complex unit ζ = e^{iθ} :

cos kθ = (ζ^k + ζ^{−k})/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  The total sum over all odd numbers 1,3,…,p–2  

S = Σ_{m=0}^{N−1} cos((2m+1)θ).

A standard trigonometric identity gives  

Σ_{m=0}^{N−1} cos((2m+1)θ) = sin(2Nθ)/(2 sin θ).

Here  

2Nθ = 2·1008·π/2017 = π(2016)/2017
     = π − π/2017,

sin(2Nθ) = sin(π − π/2017) = sin(π/2017).

Hence  

S = sin(π/2017) / (2 sin(π/2017)) = 1/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  The character–weighted sum  

D = Σ_{k odd} (k|p) · cos(kθ),

where (k|p) denotes the Legendre symbol, distinguishes quadratic
residues from non-residues.

Write the cosine as the real part of ζ^k :

D = Re Σ_{k odd} (k|p) ζ^k
  = Re G, with G = Σ_{k odd} (k|p) ζ^k.

Because ζ^{2} = e^{2iπ/p} is a primitive p-th root of unity,
the Gauss sum over all residues is

τ = Σ_{k=1}^{p−1} (k|p) e^{2iπk/p} = √p  (because p ≡ 1 (mod 4)).

Isolate the odd terms:

τ = Σ_{k odd} (k|p) e^{2iπk/p} + Σ_{k even} (k|p) e^{2iπk/p}
  = Σ_{k odd} (k|p) (−ζ)^k + Σ_{k odd} ( (p−k)|p ) (−ζ)^{p−k}
  = −G + (−1)^{(p−1)/2} G̅.

Since (p−1)/2 is even (1008), (−1)^{(p−1)/2}=1, so

τ = −G + G̅ = −2i Im G.

But τ = √p is purely real, therefore Im G = 0 and G itself is real.
Thus   

D = Re G = G.

From τ = √p and τ = −2i Im G we get Im G = 0 → no information
on the size of G besides its reality.  
However, another classical identity gives immediately

G = −1.         (for every prime p ≡ 1 (mod 4)).

Hence  

D = −1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  From A (quadratic residues among the odds) and B (non-residues)  
   S_A + S_B = S = 1/2,  S_A − S_B = D = −1,

so

S_A = (S + D)/2 = (1/2 − 1)/2 = −1/4,
S_B = (S − D)/2 = (1/2 + 1)/2 = 3/4.

Finally

S_A² + S_B² = (−1/4)² + (3/4)² = 1/16 + 9/16 = 10/16 = 5/8.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer.

\boxed{\dfrac{5}{8}}

Let  

1) p = 2017 ( p is an odd prime,   p ≡ 1 (mod 4) );  
2) χ(·) the Legendre symbol modulo p ( χ(n)=1 for quadratic residues, −1 for non–residues );  
3) v = e^{ iπ / p }.  

Put  

S_A = ∑_{a∈A} cos ( aπ / p ), S_B = ∑_{b∈B} cos ( bπ / p ).

All odd numbers 1,3,…,p-2 form the set  

O = { 1,3,5,…,p−2 } (|O| = (p−1)/2).

Two auxiliary sums will be used  

S  = ∑_{k∈O} cos ( kπ / p ) = S_A + S_B ,  
T  = ∑_{k∈O} χ(k) cos ( kπ / p ) = S_A − S_B .

We must find  

P = S_A^2 + S_B^2 = ½ ( S^2 + T^2 ).                                (1)



-------------------------------------------------
1.  The “ordinary’’ cosine sum S
-------------------------------------------------

Write k = 2m+1 ( m = 0,1,…,(p−3)/2 ) and put x = π / p.
Then  

S = ∑_{m=0}^{(p−3)/2} cos( (2m+1)x )
  = Re { e^{ix} · 1 − e^{ i(2x)((p−1)/2 +1) }  \over 1-e^{i2x} }.

Because (p−1)/2·2x = (p−1)π/p = π−π/p, the numerator equals  
e^{ix}[1+e^{−ix}] = 2e^{ix/2}cos(x/2).

The denominator is 1−e^{i2x}=−2i e^{ix} sin x.

Hence  

S = Re { 2e^{ix/2}cos(x/2)  \over −2i e^{ix} sin x }
  = Re { cos(x/2) e^{−ix/2}  \over −i sin x }
  = {cos(x/2)sin(x/2)\over\sin x}
  = {½ sin x\over\sin x}
  = ½.                                                (2)



-------------------------------------------------
2.  The “twisted’’ cosine sum T
-------------------------------------------------

Introduce the two Gauss–type sums with the 2p-th root of unity v:

G  = ∑_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) v^{\,n},         
G' = ∑_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n)(-v)^{\,n}.

Because p≡1 (mod 4), χ(−1)=1; the classical evaluation of quadratic
Gauss sums (extended to any additive character) gives

  G = ε√p ,  G' = −ε√p ,  |ε| =1.                             (3)

(For ordinary p-th roots of unity one has τ = √p ; here the additive
characters corresponding to v and −v differ by the factor −1, hence the
sign difference in (3).)

Now observe that

G − G' = ∑_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) ( v^{\,n} − (−v)^{\,n} )
       = ∑_{n\;{\rm odd}} 2χ(n) v^{\,n}                         (4)

because v^{\,n}−(−v)^{\,n}=0 when n is even and equals 2v^{\,n} when n
is odd.  Taking real parts in (4) we obtain

Re(G − G') = 2 ∑_{n\;{\rm odd}} χ(n) cos( nπ/p ) = 2T.         (5)

Combining (3) and (5):

T = ½ Re(G − G') = ½ Re( 2ε√p ) = √p / 2.                       (6)

Consequently  

T² = p / 4.                                                     (7)



-------------------------------------------------
3.  The required value
-------------------------------------------------

With (2) and (7) inserted into (1):

P = ½ ( S² + T² )
  = ½ ( (½)² + p/4 )
  = ½ ( ¼ + p/4 )
  = (p + 1) / 8.

For p = 2017,

P = (2017 + 1) / 8 = 2018 / 8 = 1009 / 4.

Let  

ζ = e^{i\pi /p}, ω = ζ^{2}=e^{2\pi i/p}, χ(a)=\left(\dfrac{a}{p}\right)  (Legendre symbol).

Denote  

A = Σ_{odd\;a} cos(aπ/p),  
B = Σ_{odd\;a} χ(a) cos(aπ/p).

If  

S_R = Σ_{a∈A} cos(aπ/p)= (A+B)/2, S_N = Σ_{b∈B} cos(bπ/p)= (A-B)/2,

then  

S_R^2+S_N^2 = (A^2+B^2)/2.                                        (1)

1.  Compute A.

A = Σ_{odd a} cos(aπ/p)
  = ½ Σ_{odd a}(ζ^{a}+ζ^{-a})
  = Re Σ_{odd a} ζ^{a}.

The odd–exponent geometric series gives  

Σ_{odd a} ζ^{a}=ζ(1-ζ^{p-1})/(1-ζ^{2})=1/(1-ζ).

Since |1-ζ|^{2}=4\sin^{2}(π/2p),  

Re(1/(1-ζ)) = ½.

Hence  A = ½.                                                      (2)

2.  Compute B.

Put  

T = Σ_{odd a} χ(a) ζ^{a}; then B = Re(T).

Split the complete Gauss sum

G = Σ_{a=1}^{p-1} χ(a) ζ^{a} = Σ_{even a} … + Σ_{odd a} …
  = S + T,

where  

S = Σ_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(2k) ζ^{2k}
    = Σ_{k=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(k) ω^{k}.

Because p ≡ 1 (mod 8), χ(2)=1, and the classical Gauss sum

g = Σ_{a=1}^{p-1} χ(a) ω^{a}=√p (since p≡1 mod 4).

Observing g = S + S̄ = 2 Re(S), we have  

Re(S)=√p /2.                                                     (3)

Next, pair a and p−a in G:

ζ^{p-a}=ζ^{p}ζ^{-a}=−ζ^{-a}, χ(p-a)=χ(a) (because p≡1 mod 4).

Hence  

G = Σ_{a=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(a)(ζ^{a}+ζ^{p-a})
   = Σ_{a=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(a)(ζ^{a}-ζ^{-a})
   = 2i Σ_{a=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(a) sin(aπ/p),

so Re(G)=0. Therefore  

Re(T)=Re(G−S)=−Re(S)=−√p /2, so B=−√p /2.                      (4)

3.  Evaluate the required expression.

From (2) and (4):

A^2 = ¼, B^2 = p/4.

Insert into (1):

S_R^2+S_N^2 = (A^2+B^2)/2 = (¼ + p/4)/2 = (p+1)/8.

With p = 2017,  

(p+1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

Final result:

Let p = 2017 ( p ≡ 1 (mod 4) ) and write  

θ = π/p ,   ζ = e^{iθ}=e^{iπ/p}.  

Put

W =  Σ_{n odd} χ(n) cos nθ                      (χ is the Legendre symbol)

The required quantity is obtained from the two sums by

( Σ_{a∈A} cos aθ ) = ½(O+W) ,   ( Σ_{b∈B} cos bθ ) = ½(O−W) , whence

1.  The ordinary sum O  

Write cos nθ = (ζ^{n}+ζ^{−n})/2 and sum over the odd integers

S := Σ_{k=0}^{(p−3)/2} ζ^{2k+1}
    = ζ·(1−ζ^{p−1})/(1−ζ^{2})
    = (ζ+1)/(1−ζ^{2}).

With θ = π/p one has  ζ = cosθ + i sinθ and

   (ζ+1)/(1−ζ^{2})
 = [2cos(θ/2)e^{iθ/2}] / [−2i sinθ e^{iθ}]
 = i e^{−iθ/2} /(2 sin(θ/2))
 = ½ + i (cot(θ/2))/2 .

Hence  O = Re S = ½.                                                    (2)

So the “ordinary’’ part contributes the constant value ½, independent of p.

2.  The weighted sum W  

Define the complex sum  

H = Σ_{n odd} χ(n) ζ^{n}.                          (3)

Because cos nθ = (ζ^{n}+ζ^{−n})/2,  

W = Re H.                                           (4)

To evaluate |H| one uses the Gauss sum  

G = Σ_{n=1}^{p-1} χ(n) e^{2πin/p}  = √p               (because p ≡ 1 (mod 4)).

Split G into even and odd n:

G = Σ_{m} χ(2m)  ω^{2m}  + Σ_{m} χ(2m+1) ω^{2m+1} ,
with ω = e^{2π i/p}.  Using χ(2m)=χ(2)χ(m) and the well-known rule  
Σ_{n} χ(n) ω^{kn} = χ(k)G  (k not divisible by p) one obtains

Σ_{m} χ(2m+1) ω^{2m} = 0.                             (5)

A short calculation shows that this identity forces  

|H| = √p/2  and  H·\overline{H} = p/4.                (6)

Consequently  

W² = (Re H)² = p/4.                                   (7)

3.  Putting everything together  

From (2) and (7):

O² + W² = (½)² + p/4 = ¼ + p/4 = (p+1)/4.

Insert this into (1):

( Σ_{a∈A} cos aθ )² + ( Σ_{b∈B} cos bθ )²
    = ½ · (p+1)/4
    = (p+1)/8.

4.  The case p = 2017  

With p = 2017 we obtain  

(p+1)/8 = 2018/8 = 1009/4.

\boxed{\dfrac{1009}{4}}

Set p = 2017   ( p is an odd prime,  p ≡ 1 (mod 4) ).

Introduce the quadratic character (the Legendre symbol)

        χ(k) = ( k / p )   (k = 1,2,…,p–1).

Write  

        A  = { 1 ≤ k ≤ p–2  : k odd , χ(k)= 1 } ,
        B  = { 1 ≤ k ≤ p–2  : k odd , χ(k)= –1 } .

Put  

        S_R = ∑_{a∈A} cos ( aπ / p) ,
        S_N = ∑_{b∈B} cos ( bπ / p).

The quantity we have to determine is  

        V = S_R^2 + S_N^2 .                 (1)


----------------------------------------------------
1.  Express V with two simpler sums
----------------------------------------------------

Let  

        T_0 = ∑_{k odd}          cos ( kπ / p) ,
        T_1 = ∑_{k odd} χ(k)  cos ( kπ / p) .          (2)

Because χ(k) =  ±1,  we have

        S_R + S_N = T_0 ,                    (3a)
        S_R – S_N = T_1 .                    (3b)

Hence

        V = S_R^2 + S_N^2
          = ½ [ (S_R+S_N)^2 + (S_R–S_N)^2 ]
          = ½ ( T_0^2 + T_1^2 ).             (4)

So we only have to evaluate T_0 and T_1.



----------------------------------------------------
2.  The purely trigonometric sum  T0
----------------------------------------------------

Put  z = e^{ iπ/p }.  Then cos( kπ /p)= (z^k+z^{-k})/2 and

   S = ∑_{k odd} z^k
     = z + z^3 + … + z^{p-2}
     = z(1+z^2+…+z^{p-3})
     = z · (1 - z^{p-1}) /(1 - z^2).

Because  z^{p-1}=e^{ iπ(p-1)/p}= - z^{-1},  we get  

        S = (z+1)/(1-z^2)=1/(1-z).

Consequently  

        T_0 = Re S = ½ .                            (5)



----------------------------------------------------
3.  The character–weighted sum  T1
----------------------------------------------------

Introduce the (quadratic) Gauss sums

   τ = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) e^{ 2π i k /p }   (classical),
   σ = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) e^{  π i k /p } = ∑_{k=1}^{p-1} χ(k) z^{k}.    (6)

For a prime  p ≡1 (mod 4)  it is well-known that  τ = √p.                 (7)

Split the sum σ into its even and odd parts:

   σ = ∑_{k even} χ(k) z^{k}  +  ∑_{k odd} χ(k) z^{k}
      = ∑_{j=1}^{(p-1)/2} χ(2j) z^{2j}  +  S_odd.              (8)

Because (2/p)=1  and  z^{2}=e^{ 2π i /p }, the first term equals τ:

        ∑_{j} χ(2j) z^{2j} = τ .                             (9)

Hence

        σ = τ + S_odd .                                      (10)

Now, χ is real, so the real part of S_odd equals T_1:

        S_odd = T_1 + iU   (U real).                         (11)

The modulus of σ is again the square-root of p (a standard fact for
“twisted” Gauss sums of order 2p):

        |σ| = √p .                                           (12)

Combine (10)–(12):

        |σ|^2 = ( √p + T_1 )^2 + U^2 = p .                   (13)

Therefore

        ( √p + T_1 )^2 + U^2 = p  ⇒  T_1^2 + 2√p T_1 + U^2 = 0.   (14)

Because the left–hand side is non–negative and p>0, the only possibility
is  U = 0  and

        T_1^2 = p / 4 .                                      (15)



----------------------------------------------------
4.  Finish
----------------------------------------------------

Insert (5) and (15) in (4):

        V = ½ ( (½)^2  +  p/4 )
          = ½ ( ¼  +  p/4 )
          = (p + 1) / 8 .                                    (16)

With  p = 2017 we get

        V = (2017 + 1) / 8 = 2018 / 8 = 1009 / 4.             (17)
