Step 2.  How many new elements appear at each index  
Let cₜ be the number of elements whose minimal index is t.  
Because Aₙ contains precisely the elements whose minimal index divides n,

∑_{t|n} cₜ = n for every n.

By Möbius inversion

cₜ = ∑_{d|t} μ(d)·t/d = φ(t),

so exactly φ(t) new elements appear at level t.

Step 3.  The 250–family of elements  
We only care about the divisors of 250 (=2·5³):

1, 2, 5, 10, 25, 50, 125, 250.

All elements occurring in the eight sets A_d (d | 250) are those whose
minimal index t also divides 250.
Their total number is

∑_{t|250} φ(t)= 250,

so A₂₅₀ consists of those 250 elements and nothing else.
We may label these 250 elements with the integers 1,2,…,250; elements coming from indices not dividing 250 can be labelled afterwards—they will never belong to A_d (d|250) and therefore cannot influence the maxima we have to sum.

Step 4.  Unavoidable “foreign’’ elements inside each A_d  
For any divisor d of 250 let  
S(d)  = { t : t | d } (“own’’ indices),  
T(d)  = { t | 250 : t ∤ d } (“foreign’’ indices).

To finish building A_d we must place all ∑_{t∈S(d)} φ(t)=d own elements.
If we have already placed k(d) foreign elements before that moment, the
maximum element of A_d is at least d + k(d).

How small can k(d) be?  
Foreign elements have to appear early only when they are needed for a still smaller set.

Compute the least possible k(d) successively.

•  d = 1 : k(1)=0 ⇒ max A₁ = 1.  
•  d = 2 : only t = 1,2 are needed, so k(2)=0 ⇒ max A₂ = 2.  
•  d = 5 : the element from t = 2 (needed for A₂) has already appeared, so k(5)=1 ⇒ max A₅ = 5+1 = 6.  
•  d = 10 : all indices 1,2,5,10 are own; no other foreign index is forced yet, so k(10)=0 ⇒ max A₁₀ = 10.  
•  d = 25 : the foreign indices 2 and 10 had to be placed for A₂ and A₁₀, contributing φ(2)+φ(10)=1+4=5 elements.  Hence k(25)=5 and max A₂₅ = 25+5 = 30.  
•  d = 50 : its only foreign index that must already be present is none—125 and 250 can be postponed—so k(50)=0 and max A₅₀ = 50.  
•  d = 125 : the foreign indices 2,10,50 (already required) supply 1+4+20 =25 elements, hence k(125)=25 and max A₁₂₅ = 125+25 = 150.  
•  d = 250 : all 250 required elements are its own, so max A₂₅₀ = 250.

Step 5.  Minimality and construction  
The values just obtained are simultaneously attainable: list the elements in the following order

t = 1 (1 element),  
t = 2 (1 element),  
t = 5 (4 elements),  
t = 10 (4 elements),  
t = 25 (20 elements),  
t = 50 (20 elements),  
t = 125 (100 elements),  
t = 250 (100 elements).

Under this ordering every A_d (d | 250) acquires all its own elements as early as allowed by the unavoidable foreign ones, so each maximum reaches the lower bound found above.  Any other ordering of the 250 relevant elements cannot lower any of those bounds (by the argument in Step 4), hence the following eight maxima are the minimum possible:

max A₁ = 1  
max A₂ = 2  
max A₅ = 6  
max A₁₀ = 10  
max A₂₅ = 30  
max A₅₀ = 50  
max A₁₂₅ = 150  
max A₂₅₀ = 250.

Step 6.  Required sum  
Adding them gives

1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Therefore the minimum possible value of  
∑_{d|250} max A_d is

\boxed{499}.

Step 1.  A convenient family of sets  
For every positive integer n create φ(n) distinct elements and place each of them in A_k exactly when n|k.  
Because  
 ∑_{n|k}φ(n)=k,  ∑_{n|gcd(i,j)}φ(n)=gcd(i,j),  
this construction guarantees  
 |A_k|=k and |A_i∩A_j|=gcd(i,j) for all i,j.  

Thus it suffices to decide which positive integers will represent the φ(n) elements for every divisor n of 250.  
The eight relevant blocks (one block for every divisor of 250) and their sizes are  

n :        1   2   5   10   25   50   125   250  
φ(n) :     1   1   4    4    20    20    100    100       (total 250).

Call the set of the φ(n) chosen numbers B_n.  
For every k|250 we then have  
A_k = ⋃_{n|k} B_n, and max A_k = max { max B_n : n|k }.

Step 2.  A workable assignment
Pick the 250 smallest positive integers and allot them as follows  

B_1  = {1}                               (max 1)  
B_2  = {2}                               (max 2)  
B_5  = {3,4,5,6}                         (max 6)  
B_10 = {7,8,9,10}                        (max 10)  
B_25 = {11,…,30}                        (max 30)  
B_50 = {31,…,50}                        (max 50)  
B_125= {51,…,150}                       (max 150)  
B_250= {151,…,250}                      (max 250).

The maxima of the eight desired sets are therefore  

max A_1 = 1  
max A_2 = 2  
max A_5 = 6  
max A_10 = 10  
max A_25 = 30  
max A_50 = 50  
max A_125 = 150  
max A_250 = 250,

and their sum is  

1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Step 3.  Why 499 is minimal  
Let the maxima of blocks B_50 and B_125 be x and y respectively; all other maxima are fixed at  
1,2,6,10,30 and 250, whose total is 299.

The 220 integers from 31 to 250 must accommodate the 20 elements of B_50 and the 100 elements of B_125 as well as the 100 elements of B_250.  
Without loss of generality take x ≤ y.  
All 120 elements of B_50∪B_125 are ≤ y, and the smallest such element is 31; hence the interval [31,y] contains at least 120 numbers, so

 y − 30 ≥ 120  ⇒  y ≥ 150.

Furthermore, the 20 elements of B_50 must lie in [31,x], so x−30 ≥ 20 ⇒  x ≥ 50.  
Consequently

 x + y ≥ 50 + 150 = 200,

giving the universal lower bound

 ∑_{d|250} max A_d ≥ 299 + 200 = 499.

Since the construction in Step 2 attains this bound, 499 is indeed the minimum possible value.

\boxed{499}

Step 1.  Structural description of the family (why |A_k| = k)

Because  
|A_i∩A_j| = gcd(i,j)  
we in particular have |A_k| = |A_k∩A_k| = gcd(k,k) = k ;  
so every A_k contains exactly k elements.

If an index r divides s then gcd(r,s)=r and hence

|A_r∩A_s| = |A_r| = r ,

so A_r ⊂ A_s whenever r|s.

Write, for every positive integer n,

B_n = { elements whose smallest index of membership is n } .

The sets B_n are pairwise disjoint and

A_k = ⋃_{n|k} B_n ,              k≥1                         (1)

hence                                                  (size of A_k)

k = |A_k| = ∑_{n|k} |B_n| .                            (2)

Let b(n)=|B_n|.  Relation (2) says that b * 1 = id under Dirichlet
convolution, so b = id*μ = φ.  Consequently

|B_n| = φ(n)                                           (3)

for every n.  In particular, for the divisors of 250 we have  

B₁ :1   B₂ :1   B₅ :4   B₁₀ :4   B₂₅ :20   B₅₀ :20   B₁₂₅ :100   B₂₅₀ :100.

Step 2.  A lower bound for the eight required maxima

Let M_d = max A_d for d|250.

•  d = 1,2,10,50,250  
   Here A_d already has d elements, so M_d ≥ d.

•  d = 5  
   Among the five smallest positive integers one of them
   (the element of B₂) is not in A₅, hence A₅ needs at least one
   number larger than 5.  Therefore M₅ ≥ 6.

•  d = 25  
   The five elements of B₂∪B₁₀ are not in A₂₅.
   Thus A₂₅ needs its 25 elements inside the first
   30 integers, so M₂₅ ≥ 30.

•  d = 125  
   Besides the 25 elements of B₂∪B₁₀∪B₅₀, none of which lie in A₁₂₅,
   A₁₂₅ still needs 125 elements.  Hence the first 150 integers
   are required and M₁₂₅ ≥ 150.

Collecting the unavoidable minima

M₁ ≥ 1,   M₂ ≥ 2,   M₅ ≥ 6,   M₁₀ ≥ 10,
M₂₅ ≥ 30, M₅₀ ≥ 50, M₁₂₅ ≥ 150, M₂₅₀ ≥ 250.

Therefore every admissible family of sets satisfies

∑_{d|250} M_d ≥
1+2+6+10+30+50+150+250 = 499.                        (4)

Step 3.  An explicit family attaining the bound

List the positive integers from 1 to 250 and distribute them, in the
order shown, to the eight blocks B_n (n|250) so that each B_n
receives exactly φ(n) distinct numbers:

B₁   = {  1 }                        (1 element)  
B₂   = {  2 }                        (1 element)  
B₅   = {  3, 4, 5, 6 }               (4 elements)  
B₁₀  = {  7, 8, 9,10 }               (4 elements)  
B₂₅  = { 11,…,30 }                   (20 elements)  
B₅₀  = { 31,…,50 }                   (20 elements)  
B₁₂₅ = { 51,…,150 }                  (100 elements)  
B₂₅₀ = {151,…,250 }                  (100 elements)

Define A_k by (1).  Because the blocks have the correct cardinalities
(3), the condition |A_i∩A_j| = gcd(i,j) holds for every i,j.

The eight maxima obtained from this construction are exactly the lower-
bound values:

( M₁, M₂, M₅, M₁₀, M₂₅, M₅₀, M₁₂₅, M₂₅₀ )
= ( 1,   2,   6,   10,   30,   50,   150,   250 ).

Hence equality is achieved in (4) and the minimum possible value of
Σ_{d|250} max A_d is 499.

\boxed{499}

Step 1.  A convenient way to satisfy  
|A_i| = i and |A_i ∩ A_j| = gcd(i , j) for every i , j is the following.

For every positive integer m let S_m be a set of φ(m) distinct positive integers, the different S_m being pair-wise disjoint.  
Define  
A_n = ⋃_{m | n} S_m .  

Because the classical identity Σ_{m | n} φ(m) = n holds, we have |A_n| = n.  
Since A_i ∩ A_j = ⋃_{m | gcd(i , j)} S_m, again using the same identity we get  
|A_i ∩ A_j| = Σ_{m | gcd(i , j)} φ(m) = gcd(i , j).  
Thus the condition of the problem is fulfilled.

Consequently, for the divisors d of 250 we only have to deal with the eight sets  
S_1 , S_2 , S_5 , S_10 , S_25 , S_50 , S_125 , S_250  
whose cardinalities are, respectively,  

φ(1)=1, φ(2)=1, φ(5)=4, φ(10)=4, φ(25)=20, φ(50)=20, φ(125)=100, φ(250)=100.  

Step 2.  Minimising the required sum.

Denote by L_m the largest element of S_m.  
For d | 250 let M_d be the maximum of A_d ; then

M_1   = L_1  
M_2   = max(L_1 , L_2)  
M_5   = max(L_1 , L_5)  
M_10  = max(L_1 , L_2 , L_5 , L_10)  
M_25  = max(L_1 , L_5 , L_25)  
M_50  = max(L_1 , L_2 , L_5 , L_10 , L_25 , L_50)  
M_125 = max(L_1 , L_5 , L_25 , L_125)  
M_250 = max(L_1 , L_2 , L_5 , L_10 , L_25 , L_50 , L_125 , L_250).

Because all S_m must be pair-wise disjoint, a lower bound for every L_m is imposed by simple counting:

•  S_1 needs 1 element, so L_1 ≥ 1.  
   Take S_1 = {1}, hence L_1 = 1.

•  S_2 needs 1 new element, so L_2 ≥ 2.  
   Take S_2 = {2}, hence L_2 = 2.

Up to now the numbers 1 and 2 are used.

•  S_5 needs 4 new elements besides 1 and 2.  
   Only 3 integers ≤5 remain (3,4,5), so at least one element must be 6.  
   Thus L_5 ≥ 6.  
   Choose S_5 = {3,4,5,6} giving L_5 = 6.

Numbers 1–6 are now used.

•  S_10 needs 4 fresh elements; the smallest possible choice is 7,8,9,10,  
   hence L_10 ≥ 10 and we set S_10 = {7,8,9,10}.

Numbers 1–10 are used.

•  S_25 needs 20 further distinct numbers; the smallest available block
   is 11–30, so L_25 ≥ 30.  
   Take S_25 = {11,12,…,30}.

Numbers 1–30 are used.

•  S_50 needs another 20 numbers; the least we can do is 31–50,
   giving L_50 ≥ 50.  
   Set S_50 = {31,32,…,50}.

Numbers 1–50 are used.

•  S_125 needs 100 numbers; the least interval is 51–150,
   hence L_125 ≥ 150.  
   Put S_125 = {51,52,…,150}.

•  S_250 needs the final 100 numbers; choose 151–250,
   so L_250 = 250.

Step 3.  The maxima of the eight A_d are therefore

M_1   = 1  
M_2   = 2  
M_5   = 6  
M_10  = 10  
M_25  = 30  
M_50  = 50  
M_125 = 150  
M_250 = 250.

Adding them gives

1 + 2 + 6 + 10 + 30 + 50 + 150 + 250 = 499.

Step 4.  Optimality.

Each L_m achieved above attains its unavoidable lower bound, hence every M_d is as small as possible.  Therefore no other construction can yield a smaller value of the required sum.

Final answer:

\boxed{499}

Let the roots of  

x³ − x² − 5x − 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.  
Denote  

S₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
S₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = −5  
S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1.

We need  

P = (x₁² − 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² − 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² − 4x₃x₁ + x₁²).

1.  Factor each quadratic.  
   Solve k² − 4k + 1 = 0 ⇒ k = 2 ± √3.  
   Call a = 2+√3, b = 2−√3 (ab = 1).  
   Then x² − 4xy + y² = (x − ay)(x − by).

   Hence  
   P = ∏cyclic (xᵢ − a xⱼ)(xᵢ − b xⱼ)  
     = F(a)·F(b)   with   F(k) = (x₁ − kx₂)(x₂ − kx₃)(x₃ − kx₁).

2.  Express F(k) through the symmetric sums.  
   Expanding gives  

   F(k) = (1 − k³)S₃ − k(x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁) + k²(x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁²).

   Put  
   C₁ = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁,  
   C₂ = x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁²,  
   Δ = (x₁−x₂)(x₂−x₃)(x₃−x₁).

   Then  
   C₁ + C₂ = S₁S₂ − 3S₃ = −8,  
   C₁ − C₂ = Δ.

   So  

   F(k) = 1 − k³ − 4(k² − k) − (Δ/2)(k + k²)  
        = 1 + 4k − 4k² − k³ − (Δ/2)(k + k²).

3.  For k satisfying k² − 4k + 1 = 0, replace k² and k³:

   k² = 4k − 1,    k³ = 15k − 4,

   whence  

   F(k) = 9 − 27k − (Δ/2)(5k − 1).

   Let δ = Δ/2.  The discriminant of the cubic is

   Δ² = 404  ⇒  δ² = 101.

4.  Compute F(a)F(b).

   Write α(k)=9−27k,  β(k)=5k−1, so F(k)=α(k)−δβ(k).  
   Using a+b=4, ab=1:

   α(a)α(b) = 9²(1−3a)(1−3b) = 81(−2) = −162  
   β(a)β(b) = (5a−1)(5b−1) = 6  
   α(a)β(b)+α(b)β(a) = 0.

   Therefore  

   P = F(a)F(b)  
     = α(a)α(b) + δ² β(a)β(b)  
     = (−162) + 101·6  
     = −162 + 606  
     = 444.

Hence  

\boxed{444}

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

  f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.  
Denote the required product by  

  P=(x₁²-4x₁x₂+x₂²)(x₂²-4x₂x₃+x₃²)(x₃²-4x₃x₁+x₁²).

----------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Re-express every factor in terms of the root that does NOT appear
----------------------------------------------------------------
For the first factor put A=x₁ , B=x₂ , C=x₃.  
Because A+B = 1−C   and   AB = −5−C+ C² (from the relations
x₁+x₂+x₃=1 , x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁=-5) we have

 x₁²-4x₁x₂+x₂² = (A+B)²-6AB = (1-C)²-6(−5−C+C²)
        = 31+4C-5C².

Thus
 x₁²-4x₁x₂+x₂² = 31+4x₃-5x₃²,
 x₂²-4x₂x₃+x₃² = 31+4x₁-5x₁²,
 x₃²-4x₃x₁+x₁² = 31+4x₂-5x₂².

Therefore

  P = ∏_{i=1}^{3} (31+4x_{i}-5x_{i}^{2}).             (1)

Let  

  h(x)= -5x^{2}+4x+31.

Then (1) becomes  

  P = h(x₁)h(x₂)h(x₃).                                (2)

----------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Interpret the product as a resultant
----------------------------------------------------------------
For a cubic p(x) with leading coefficient 1 and any polynomial h(x)
of degree 2,

  Res(p,h)=∏_{p(r)=0} h(r).

Here p(x)=f(x) and hence

  P = Res(f,h).                                       (3)

----------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Compute Res(f,h) via the Sylvester determinant
----------------------------------------------------------------
Write the Sylvester matrix of f and h (rows built from their
coefficients):

  |  1  −1  −5  −1   0 |
  |  0   1  −1  −5  −1 |
  | −5   4  31   0   0 |
  |  0  −5   4  31   0 |
  |  0   0  −5   4  31 |

Row–operation reductions (only adding multiples of other rows, which
leave the determinant unchanged) give an upper–triangular matrix with
diagonal entries 1, 1, 5, 4 and 111/5.  
Hence

  determinant = 1·1·5·4·(111/5)=444.

Because of (3),

  P = 444.

----------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Result
----------------------------------------------------------------
\boxed{444}

Let the roots of  
x³ − x² − 5x − 1 = 0  
be x₁ , x₂ , x₃ .

1. Elementary symmetric sums  
S₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = 1  
S₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = −5  
S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1

2. Write the desired product in a convenient form  
Set F(a , b) = a² − 4ab + b² = (a − λb)(a − λ⁻¹b) with  
λ = 2 + √3 , λ⁻¹ = 2 − √3 (because λ + λ⁻¹ = 4, λλ⁻¹ = 1).

Hence  

P = ∏ cyclic F(x_i , x_{i+1})  
  = ∏ (x_i − λx_{i+1}) ∏ (x_i − λ⁻¹x_{i+1})  
  = G(λ) · G(λ⁻¹)  

where  
G(t) = (x₁ − tx₂)(x₂ − tx₃)(x₃ − tx₁).

3. Expand G(t) once:

G(t) = S₃ − t Σ x_i²x_{i+1} + t² Σ x_ix_{i+1}² − t³S₃  
     = 1 − tP₁ + t²P₂ − t³,  

with  
P₁ = Σ x_i²x_{i+1}, P₂ = Σ x_ix_{i+1}².

4. Useful relations for P₁ , P₂  

Sum: P₁ + P₂ = S₁S₂ − 3S₃ = (1)(−5) − 3(1) = −8.  

Difference: P₁ − P₂ = −(x₁ − x₂)(x₂ − x₃)(x₃ − x₁).  
The square of this difference equals the discriminant Δ of the cubic:  
Δ = 404 = 4·101 ⇒ (P₁ − P₂)² = 404.

Therefore  

P₁P₂ = ((P₁ + P₂)² − (P₁ − P₂)²)/4  
      = (64 − 404)/4 = −85,  

and  

P₁² + P₂² = (P₁ + P₂)² − 2P₁P₂ = 64 + 170 = 234.

5. Compute G(λ)G(λ⁻¹)

Write G(t) = A₀ + A₁t + A₂t² + A₃t³ with  
(A₀,A₁,A₂,A₃) = (1, −P₁, P₂, −1).

Then  
G(t)G(t⁻¹) = Σ_{k=0}^3 Σ_{j=0}^3 A_kA_j t^{\,k−j}  
           = S₀ + S₁(t + t⁻¹) + S₂(t² + t⁻²) + S₃(t³ + t⁻³),

with  
S₀ = 2 + P₁² + P₂²  
S₁ = −(P₁ + P₂ + P₁P₂)  
S₂ =  P₁ + P₂  
S₃ = −1.

For t = λ we have  
t + t⁻¹ = 4, t² + t⁻² = 14, t³ + t⁻³ = 52.

Therefore  

P = G(λ)G(λ⁻¹)  
  = (2 + P₁² + P₂²)  
    + (−(P₁ + P₂ + P₁P₂))·4  
    + (P₁ + P₂)·14  
    + (−1)·52.

Insert the found values:

P = (2 + 234) + (−(−8 − 85))·4 + (−8)·14 − 52  
  = 236 + 93·4 − 112 − 52  
  = 236 + 372 − 164  
  = 444.

Hence

(x₁² − 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² − 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² − 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let the roots of  
f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1=0  
be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
   s₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
   s₂ = x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ = -5  
   s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1

2.  Write the desired factors in a convenient way.  
   x_i^{2}-4x_i x_j+x_j^{2} = (x_i-(2+\sqrt3)x_j)(x_i-(2-\sqrt3)x_j)  
   Denote α = 2+√3 , β = 2-√3 (αβ = 1, α+β = 4).

   Then  
   P = ∏_{cyc}(x_i^{2}-4x_i x_j+x_j^{2}) = Q(α)·Q(β)  
   where Q(k) = (x₁-kx₂)(x₂-kx₃)(x₃-kx₁).

3.  Expand Q(k).  
   Put  
   T₁ = x₁x₂^{2}+x₂x₃^{2}+x₃x₁^{2},   
   T₂ = x₁^{2}x₂+x₂^{2}x₃+x₃^{2}x₁,   
   Δ = (x₁-x₂)(x₂-x₃)(x₃-x₁).

   Then  
   Q(k) = s₃(1-k^{3}) + k^{2}T₂ - kT₁.              (1)

   Useful identities:  
   T₁+T₂ = s₁s₂-3s₃ = (1)(-5)-3 = -8,  
   T₂-T₁ = -Δ  ⇒  T₂ = -4-Δ/2 ,  T₁ = -4+Δ/2.

   Substituting these and s₃ = 1 in (1),

   Q(k) = -k^{3}+(-4-Δ/2)k^{2}+(4-Δ/2)k+1.          (2)

4.  Discriminant (needed for Δ²).  
   For x³ - x² -5x -1, the discriminant is

   D = b^{2}c^{2}-4ac^{3}-4b^{3}d-27a^{2}d^{2}+18abcd  
     = 25 + 500 - 4 - 27 - 90 = 404.

   Hence Δ² = 404 ⇒ Δ = ±2√101.

5.  Reduce Q(k) modulo k²-4k+1 (the minimal polynomial of α,β).  
   For a root of k²-4k+1=0 we have k² = 4k-1 and k³ = 15k-4.

   Using (2):

   Q(k) = -(15k-4)+(-4-Δ/2)(4k-1)+(4-Δ/2)k+1  
        = a k + b,

   where  
   a = -15 + 4(-4-Δ/2)+(4-Δ/2) = -27 - (5/2)Δ,  
   b =  4  -(-4-Δ/2)          +1 =  9 +  (Δ/2).

6.  Compute the product

   P = Q(α)Q(β) = (aα+b)(aβ+b)  
     = a^{2}αβ + ab(α+β) + b^{2}  
     = a^{2}·1 + ab·4 + b^{2}.                 (αβ=1, α+β=4)

   With Δ = d for brevity,

   a = -27 - 5d/2 ,    b = 9 + d/2.

   a²      = 729 + 135d + 25d²/4  
   4ab     = -972 -144d - 5d²  
   b²      =  81 +   9d +   d²/4

   Summing gives  
   P = (-162) + (0)d + (3/2)d²  
     = -162 + (3/2)(404)  
     = -162 + 606  
     = 444.

   (The sign of Δ cancels, so the result is independent of the ordering of the roots.)

Final answer: \boxed{444}

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

 f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1=0  

be x₁ ,x₂ ,x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
 s₁ =x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
 s₂ =x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ = –5  
 s₃ =x₁x₂x₃ = 1

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Factorising the quadratic expression  

 x_i^{2}-4x_i x_j +x_j^{2}=(x_i-αx_j)(x_i-βx_j)  

where α,β are the two real roots of t²–4t+1=0, namely  

 α = 2+√3 , β = 2-√3 , α+β = 4 , αβ = 1.

Hence

P ≡ ∏_{cyc}(x_i^{2}-4x_i x_j+x_j^{2})
  = ∏_{cyc}(x_i-αx_j)·∏_{cyc}(x_i-βx_j)
  = r(α)·r(β)

with  

 r(t) = (x₁-tx₂)(x₂-tx₃)(x₃-tx₁).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  r(t) as a cubic in t  

Expanding once,

r(t)=s₃ - S₁ t + S₂ t² - s₃ t³,

where  
 S₁ = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁,  
 S₂ = x₁²x₃ + x₂²x₁ + x₃²x₂.

Notice S₁+S₂ = Σ_{i≠j}x_i²x_j
              = s₁s₂ - 3s₃ = (1)(–5) - 3(1) = –8.      (1)

Further,

S₁–S₂ = –(x₁–x₂)(x₂–x₃)(x₃–x₁).            (2)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  The discriminant supplies |S₁–S₂|

For the monic cubic x³+ax²+bx+c the discriminant equals  
Δ = (x₁–x₂)²(x₂–x₃)²(x₃–x₁)².

With a= –1, b= –5, c= –1:

Δ = a²b² –4b³ –4a³c –27c² +18abc  
   = 25 +500 –4 –27 –90 = 404.

Since x₁<x₃<x₂, the actual Vandermonde product is negative,
so from (2)

 S₁–S₂ = √404.                                   (3)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  S₁ and S₂ individually  

From (1) and (3):

 S₁ = (–8 + √404)/2, S₂ = (–8 – √404)/2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Evaluate r(α)·r(β)

Write r(t)=a t³ + b t² + c t + d with  

 a= –s₃ = –1, b = S₂, c = –S₁, d = s₃ = 1.

Because α,β satisfy t² = 4t –1, reduce the cubic:

α³ = 15α –4, β³ = 15β –4.

Thus  

r(α) = aα³ + bα² + cα + d = P₁ α + Q₁,  
r(β) = P₁ β + Q₁,

where  

P₁ = 15a + 4b + c = –15 + 4S₂ – S₁,  
Q₁ = –4a – b + d = 5 – S₂.

Hence  

r(α) r(β) = P₁²(αβ) + P₁Q₁(α+β) + Q₁²  
           = P₁² + 4P₁Q₁ + Q₁².

Substituting S₁,S₂:

P₁ = (–54 – 5√404)/2, Q₁ = (18 + √404)/2.

Compute

P = P₁² + 4P₁Q₁ + Q₁²
  = [ (–54 – 5√404)² + (18 + √404)² + 4(–54 –5√404)(18 + √404) ] /4
  = (–648 + 6·404) /4
  = 3(–108 + 404)/2
  = 444.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
7.  Result  

Therefore

(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let the roots of  

x³ − x² − 5x − 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
   s₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
   s₂ = x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ = −5  
   s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1  

2.  Quantity to compute  
   P = (x₁² − 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² − 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² − 4x₃x₁ + x₁²).

   Each quadratic factor can be written as  
   x_i² − 4x_ix_{i+1}+x_{i+1}² = (x_i − αx_{i+1})(x_i − βx_{i+1}),  
   where α = 2+√3 and β = 2−√3, with αβ = 1.

   Hence  
   P = F(α)F(β) with F(t) = (x₁ − t x₂)(x₂ − t x₃)(x₃ − t x₁).

3.  Express F(t) in symmetric sums.  
   Put  

   A = x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁²,   
   B = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁,  

   then  
   F(t) = s₃ − tA + t²B − t³s₃.         (1)

   Two useful identities (verified by expansion):

   A + B = s₁s₂ − 3s₃,                         (2)  
   A − B = (x₁−x₂)(x₂−x₃)(x₃−x₁) ≡ Δ.         (3)

4.  Numerical data  
   From (2): A + B = 1(−5) − 3(1) = −8.  
   The discriminant of the cubic is  

   Δ² = (x₁−x₂)²(x₂−x₃)²(x₃−x₁)² = 404,  

   so Δ = ±2√101.

   Define  
   S = s₁s₂ − 3s₃ = −8.

   From (2)(3):  
   A = (S+Δ)/2 , B = (S−Δ)/2.

   Write coefficients  

   a = (S+Δ)/2, b = (S−Δ)/2.

   Then (1) becomes  

   F(t) = 1 − a t + b t² − t³.              (4)

5.  Compute P = F(α)F(β) with αβ = 1, α+β = 4.  

   Put K(t) = 1 − a t + b t² − t³.  
   Expanding K(α)K(β) and using α+β = 4, α²+β² = 14, α³+β³ = 52, αβ = 1, one obtains  

   K(α)K(β) = a² + b² − 4ab + 10(a+b) − 50 + 2  
            = (3Δ² − S²)/2 + 10S − 50  
            = (3Δ² − 64)/2 − 80 − 50  
            = (3Δ² − 324)/2.  

   Substitute Δ² = 404:  

   K(α)K(β) = (3·404 − 324)/2 = (1212 − 324)/2 = 888/2 = 444.

6.  Therefore  

   P = 444.

Let  

x₁ , x₂ , x₃  be the three (distinct) roots of  

  f(x)=x³−x²−5x−1=0 .

Denote the elementary symmetric sums  

s₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ ,  s₂ = x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ ,  s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ .

From the monic cubic we have immediately  

s₁ = 1 ,  s₂ = −5 ,  s₃ = 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Express the desired quantity as a symmetric polynomial
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Define  

P = (x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²)(x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃²)(x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁²).

Because each factor is homogeneous of degree 2, P is a symmetric homogeneous
polynomial of degree 6 in the roots.  
For three variables every symmetric polynomial of degree 6 can be written as
a linear combination of

s₁⁶ , s₁⁴s₂ , s₁²s₂² , s₂³ , s₁³s₃ , s₁s₂s₃ , s₃².

Hence let  

P = a s₁⁶ + b s₁⁴s₂ + c s₁²s₂² + d s₂³
    + e s₁³s₃ + f s₁s₂s₃ + g s₃².        (★)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Determine the coefficients a,…,g
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Evaluate P and the right–hand side of (★) for convenient triples (x,y,z)
to obtain linear equations for a,…,g.

a) (1,0,0): P=0, s₁=1,s₂=0,s₃=0 ⇒ a=0.

b) (1,1,0): P=−2, s₁=2,s₂=1,s₃=0  
        ⇒ 16b+4c+d=−2.                     (1)

c) (1,−1,0): P=6, s₁=0,s₂=−1,s₃=0  
        ⇒ d(−1)³ = 6 ⇒ d = −6.            (2)

Equation (1) with d=−6 gives 4b+c = 1.          (3)

d) (1,1,1): P=−8, s₁=3,s₂=3,s₃=1  
        ⇒ 243b+81c−162+27e+9f+g = −8.       (4)

e) (1,2,3): P=−66, s₁=6,s₂=11,s₃=6  
        ⇒ 14256b+4356c+1296e+396f+36g = 7920.   (5)

f) (1,1,2): P=−18, s₁=4,s₂=5,s₃=2  
        ⇒ 1280b+400c+128e+40f+4g = 732.         (6)

g) (1,2,2): P=−72, s₁=5,s₂=8,s₃=4  
        ⇒ 5000b+1600c+500e+160f+16g = 3000.      (7)

Solving the linear system (3)–(7) gives  

a = 0,  b = 0,  c = 1,  d = −6,  e = −6,  f = 40,  g = −125.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  P in terms of s₁,s₂,s₃
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Substituting the coefficients into (★):

P = s₁²s₂² − 6s₂³ − 6s₁³s₃ + 40s₁s₂s₃ − 125s₃².      (♣)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Insert the actual symmetric sums of the cubic
--------------------------------------------------------------------
With s₁ = 1 , s₂ = −5 , s₃ = 1 :

s₁²s₂² = 1·25 = 25  
−6s₂³   = −6(−125) = 750  
−6s₁³s₃ = −6(1)(1) = −6  
40s₁s₂s₃ = 40(1)(−5)(1) = −200  
−125s₃²  = −125(1) = −125

Add them:

25 + 750 − 6 − 200 − 125 = 444.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Therefore

(x₁² − 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² − 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² − 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) = \boxed{444}.

Let the roots of  

  x³ − x² − 5x − 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.  
Set  

  s₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = 1  
  s₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = −5  
  s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = −1.

We have to evaluate  

  P = (x₁² − 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² − 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² − 4x₃x₁ + x₁²).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.   Factor every quadratic factor

For any a , b  

  a² − 4ab + b² = (a − λb)(a − μb) ,

where λ = 2 + √3 and μ = 2 − √3 (λ + μ = 4 , λμ = 1).

Hence  

  P = ∏cyc (x_i − λx_j) ∏cyc (x_i − μx_j)  
    = Q(λ) Q(μ)

where, for any t,  

  Q(t) = (x₁ − tx₂)(x₂ − tx₃)(x₃ − tx₁).
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.   Express Q(t) with symmetric polynomials

Write Q(t) in the form U(t) + V(t)Δ, where  

  Δ = (x₁ − x₂)(x₂ − x₃)(x₃ − x₁).

Expanding once gives  

  Q(t) = (1 − t³)s₃ + ½ t(t−1)(s₁s₂ − 3s₃) + ½ t(1+t)Δ.

Using the numerical values s₁ = 1, s₂ = −5, s₃ = −1 (so s₁s₂ − 3s₃ = −2) we get

  U(t) = t³ − t² + t − 1 = (t² + 1)(t − 1),  
  V(t) = ½ t(1 + t).

Thus  

  Q(t) = U(t) + V(t)Δ.
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.   Compute U(λ)U(μ) and V(λ)V(μ)

Because λ and μ satisfy λ + μ = 4, λμ = 1:

a)  U(t) = t³ − t² + t − 1 = 4(3t − 1) using t² = 4t − 1.  
Hence U(λ) = 4(3λ − 1), U(μ) = 4(3μ − 1)

  (3λ − 1)(3μ − 1) = 9λμ − 3(λ + μ) + 1 = 9 − 12 + 1 = −2,

so U(λ)U(μ) = 16·(−2) = −32.

b)  V(t) = ½ t(1 + t); V(λ)V(μ) = ¼ λμ(λ + 1)(μ + 1)  
  = ¼ ·1·( (λ + μ) + 1 + λμ ) = ¼ (4 + 1 + 1) = ¾·2 = 3⁄2.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.   Discriminant (Δ²)

For a monic cubic x³ + Ax² + Bx + C the discriminant is  

  D = A²B² − 4B³ − 4A³C − 27C² + 18ABC.

With A = −1, B = −5, C = −1:

  D = (−1)²(−5)² − 4(−5)³ − 4(−1)³(−1) − 27(−1)² + 18(−1)(−5)(−1)  
    = 25 + 500 − 4 − 27 − 90 = 404.

But D = Δ², so Δ² = 404.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.   Assemble P

Because the coefficient of Δ in Q(λ)Q(μ) cancels (one can check U(λ)V(μ) + U(μ)V(λ) = 0), we have

  P = U(λ)U(μ) + V(λ)V(μ)Δ²  
    = (−32) + (3/2)(404)  
    = −32 + 606  
    = 574.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final result
\boxed{574}

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
   Because the polynomial is monic,

   s₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = 1            
   s₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = –5     
   s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1

2.  A convenient factorisation  
   For any two variables

   x² – 4xy + y² = (x – αy)(x – βy) ,

   where α = 2 + √3 ,  β = 2 – √3 (so αβ = 1).

   Hence the required product is

   E = Πcyclic (x_i² – 4x_i x_{i+1} + x_{i+1}²)
     = Πcyclic (x_i – αx_{i+1})(x_i – βx_{i+1})
     = F(α) · F(β),

   with
   F(k) = (x₁ – kx₂)(x₂ – kx₃)(x₃ – kx₁).

3.  Express F(k) in symmetric form  
   Expanding once gives

   F(k) = s₃(1 – k³) – kA + k²B,

   where
   A = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁,     
   B = x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁².

4.  Find A and B via the discriminant  
   First, A + B = s₁s₂ – 3s₃ = (1)(–5) – 3 = –8.

   The cubic discriminant is

   Δ² = (x₁ – x₂)²(x₂ – x₃)²(x₃ – x₁)²
       = b²c² – 4ac³ – 4b³d – 27a²d² + 18abcd
       = (–1)²(–5)² – 4(1)(–5)³ – 4(–1)³(–1)
         – 27(1)²(–1)² + 18(1)(–1)(–5)(–1)
       = 404.

   A – B = √404 ≡ R.                 (Because A – B equals –(x₁–x₂)(x₂–x₃)(x₃–x₁).)

   Solving gives

   A = (R – 8)/2,        B = –(8 + R)/2,     R = √404.

5.  Evaluate F(α) and F(β)  
   α satisfies α² = 4α – 1 and α³ = 15α – 4 (the same for β).

   Substituting and simplifying,

   F(α) = (5 – B) + α(–15 – A + 4B),
   F(β) = (5 – B) + β(–15 – A + 4B).

   Write C = 5 – B, D = –15 – A + 4B; then

   E = F(α)F(β) = (C + αD)(C + βD)
                = C² + CD(α + β) + D²(αβ)
                = C² + 4CD + D², since α + β = 4 and αβ = 1.

6.  Compute C, D and E  

   C = 5 – B = 5 + (8 + R)/2 = 9 + R/2,
   D = –15 – A + 4B = –27 – 5R/2.

   Now  

   C²  = 81 + 9R + R²/4  
   D²  = 729 + 135R + 25R²/4  
   4CD = –972 – 144R – 5R²

   Adding:  

   E = C² + 4CD + D²  
     = (81 – 972 + 729) + (9 – 144 + 135)R + (¼ – 5 + 25/4)R²  
     = –162 + 0·R + (3/2)R².

   Because R² = 404,

   E = –162 + (3/2)(404) = –162 + 606 = 444.

Therefore

\boxed{444}

Let the roots of  
x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  
be x₁ , x₂ , x₃ (so x₁≠x₂≠x₃).

1.  Express each factor through a parameter t  

   x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂² = (x₁ – αx₂)(x₁ – βx₂)  
   where α,β are the roots of t² – 4t + 1 = 0  
   (α = 2 + √3,   β = 2 – √3,   α+β = 4,   αβ = 1).

   Hence  
   P =(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁²)  
      = Q(α)·Q(β),

   where Q(t)= (x₁ – t x₂)(x₂ – t x₃)(x₃ – t x₁).

2.  Write Q(t) as a cubic polynomial  

   Q(t)= x₁x₂x₃ – t(x₁x₃²+x₂²x₃+x₁²x₂)  
         + t²(x₂x₃²+x₁²x₃+x₁x₂²) – t³x₁x₂x₃  
        = 1 – S₁ t + S₂ t² – t³,

   where (using s₁=x₁+x₂+x₃=1, s₂=x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁=–5, s₃=x₁x₂x₃=1)

      S₁+S₂ = s₁s₂ – 3s₃ = (1)(–5) – 3 = –8.     (1)

   Also Q(1)= (x₁–x₂)(x₂–x₃)(x₃–x₁) ≡ D,  
   so  S₂–S₁ = D.                                (2)

   Solving (1) and (2):  
      S₁ = (–8 – D)/2,     S₂ = (–8 + D)/2.

   Thus Q(t)= 1 + (8+D) t/2 + (–8+D) t²/2 – t³.

3.  Compute Q(α)Q(β) by reducing Q(t) modulo g(t)=t²–4t+1.

   Because t² ≡ 4t–1 and t³ ≡15t–4 (mod g),

     Q(t) ≡ p t + q (mod g) with  
     p = –15 + 4·[(–8+D)/2] + (8+D)/2 = (5D – 54)/2,  
     q = 5 – (–8+D)/2 = (18 – D)/2.

   For αβ=1, α+β=4,
     Q(α)Q(β)= p² + 4pq + q².

   Setting p=(5D–54)/2 and q=(18–D)/2 gives  
     Q(α)Q(β)= (3/2)(D² – 108).

4.  D² is the discriminant Δ of the cubic.  
   For x³ – x² – 5x – 1,  

        Δ = b²c² – 4c³ – 4b³d – 27d² + 18bcd  
           = (–1)²(–5)² – 4(–5)³ – 4(–1)³(–1) – 27(–1)² + 18(–1)(–5)(–1)  
           = 25 + 500 – 4 – 27 – 90 = 404.

   Hence D² = 404.

5.  Finally  

   P = (3/2)(404 – 108) = (3/2)(296) = 444.

Let the three distinct roots of  

  x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
 s₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ = 1  
 s₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = –5  
 s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1

2.  A convenient factorisation  
For any two numbers u , v  

 u² – 4uv + v² = (u – a v)(u – b v)  

where a = 2 + √3 , b = 2 – √3  (ab = 1).

Thus  
(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁²)
 = ∏cyclic (xᵢ – a xⱼ)(xᵢ – b xⱼ)
 = F(a)·F(b)           (with b = 1/a)

where  

 F(t) = (x₁ – t x₂)(x₂ – t x₃)(x₃ – t x₁).

3.  Write F(t) in terms of the roots  
Put  

 C₁ = x₁x₂x₃  (= s₃ = 1)  
 E₁ = x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁ + x₁²x₂  
 E₂ = x₂x₃² + x₃x₁² + x₁x₂².  

Then  

 F(t) = C₁ – tE₁ + t²E₂ – t³C₁.

4.  F(t)F(1/t)  
Multiplying F(t) by F(1/t) and grouping by powers of t + 1/t gives  

 F(t)F(1/t) = A₀ + (t+1/t)B₁ + (t²+1/t²)B₂ + (t³+1/t³)B₃

with  

 A₀ = 2C₁² + E₁² + E₂²  
 B₁ = –[ C₁(E₁ + E₂) + E₁E₂ ]  
 B₂ =  C₁(E₁ + E₂)  
 B₃ = –C₁².

Because ab = 1, we need t = a and 1/t = b; hence t + 1/t = a + b = 4.  
Consequently  

 t² + 1/t² = 4² – 2 = 14  
 t³ + 1/t³ = 4³ – 3·4 = 52.

Thus  

 F(a)F(b) = A₀ + 4B₁ + 14B₂ + 52B₃
      = (2 + E₁² + E₂²) – 4[ C₁(E₁ + E₂) + E₁E₂ ] + 14[ C₁(E₁ + E₂) ] – 52.

With C₁ = 1 this simplifies to  

 F(a)F(b) = –66 – 6E₁E₂.  (★)

5.  Evaluate E₁ + E₂ and E₁E₂  

a) E₁ + E₂  
 E₁ + E₂ = Σ xᵢxⱼ(xᵢ + xⱼ)  
      = s₁s₂ – 3s₃ = (1)(–5) – 3(1) = –8.

b) E₁E₂  
  Introduce p = x₁x₂ , q = x₂x₃ , r = x₃x₁.  
  Then p + q + r = s₂ = –5, pq + qr + rp = s₃s₁ = 1, pqr = s₃² = 1.  
  A straightforward expansion gives  

 E₁E₂ = p³ + q³ + r³ + s₃(x₁³ + x₂³ + x₃³) + 3s₃².

 • p³ + q³ + r³ = (p+q+r)³ – 3(p+q+r)(pq+qr+rp) + 3pqr  
          = (–5)³ – 3(–5)(1) + 3(1) = –125 + 15 + 3 = –107.  
 • x₁³ + x₂³ + x₃³, by Newton’s identities, equals  
   s₁P₂ – s₂P₁ + 3s₃ = (1)(11) – (–5)(1) + 3 = 11 + 5 + 3 = 19.  

Therefore  

 E₁E₂ = –107 + 1·19 + 3 = –85.

6.  Final computation  
Insert E₁E₂ = –85 into (★):

 F(a)F(b) = –66 – 6(–85) = –66 + 510 = 444.

Hence  

(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) = 444.

Let the roots of  

  f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
   e₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
   e₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = -5  
   e₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1  

2.  Rewrite the required factors  
   x_i^{2}-4x_i x_j + x_j^{2} = (x_i-α x_j)(x_i-β x_j)  
   where α , β are the roots of t²-4t+1=0, i.e.  
   α = 2+√3 , β = 2-√3 , and α+β = 4 , αβ = 1.

   Hence  

 P = Π_{cyclic}(x_i^{2}-4x_i x_j + x_j^{2})  
   = g(α) · g(β) with  

   g(t) = (x₁-tx₂)(x₂-tx₃)(x₃-tx₁).

3.  Expand g(t).  Put  

  A = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁ ,  
  B = x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁² ,  
  D = (x₁-x₂)(x₂-x₃)(x₃-x₁).

   Then  
  g(t)= e₃ − A t + B t² − e₃ t³.

   Now  
  A+B = e₁e₂ − 3e₃ , B−A = D.

   Hence  
  A = (e₁e₂ − 3e₃ − D)/2, B = (e₁e₂ − 3e₃ + D)/2.

4.  Discriminant to find D²  

 Δ = D² = e₁²e₂² − 4e₂³ − 4e₁³e₃ − 27e₃² + 18e₁e₂e₃.

   With (e₁,e₂,e₃) = (1,−5,1):

 Δ = 1·25 − 4(−125) − 4·1·1 − 27·1 + 18·1·(−5)  
   = 25 + 500 − 4 − 27 − 90 = 404.

   Hence D² = 404 (so D = ±2√101).

5.  Coefficients of g(t)

 c₀ = e₃ = 1, c₁ = −A = 4 + D/2,  
 c₂ =  B  = −4 + D/2, c₃ = −e₃ = −1.

6.  Needed power sums of α,β  

 S₁ = α+β = 4, S₂ = α²+β² = 14, S₃ = α³+β³ = 52, αβ = 1.

7.  Compute g(α)g(β)

 Using  
 g(α)g(β)=c₀² + c₀c₁S₁ + c₀c₂S₂ + c₀c₃S₃   
      + c₁c₂S₁ + c₁c₃S₂ + c₂c₃S₁   
      + c₁²(αβ) + c₂²(αβ)² + c₃²(αβ)³

 and substituting numbers gives  

 g(α)g(β) = –162 + (3/2)D² = –162 + (3/2)(404) = –162 + 606 = 444.

8.  Therefore  

 P = (x₁^{2}-4x₁x₂+x₂^{2})(x₂^{2}-4x₂x₃+x₃^{2})(x₃^{2}-4x₃x₁+x₁^{2}) = 444.

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

  f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1=0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃ .  
Set

  S₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ , S₂ = x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ , S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ .

Because f(x)=x³−x²−5x−1 is monic, Vieta’s relations give  

  S₁ = 1 , S₂ = −5 , S₃ = 1 .

------------------------------------------------
1.  Re-express each factor  

For the pair (x₁ ,x₂) :

 x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²  
 = (x₁+x₂)²−6x₁x₂  [since (x₁+x₂)² = x₁²+2x₁x₂+x₂²].

So for every cyclic pair we have

 x_i²−4x_ix_j+x_j² = (x_i+x_j)²−6x_ix_j.

Using S₁= x₁+x₂+x₃ and S₂, write

 x₁+x₂ = S₁−x₃ = 1−x₃,  x₁x₂ = S₂−x₃( S₁−x₃).

Hence

 x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²  
 = (1−x₃)² − 6[ S₂ − x₃(1−x₃) ]  
 = 1−2x₃+x₃² −6S₂ +6x₃(1−x₃)  
 = −5x₃² +4x₃ +31.                 (because S₁=1 , S₂=−5)

Define

 Q(t)= −5t² + 4t + 31.

Then

 x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂² = Q(x₃),  
 x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃² = Q(x₁),  
 x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁² = Q(x₂).

------------------------------------------------
2.  Desired product in terms of Q

Therefore

P = (x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²)(x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃²)(x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁²)  
  = Q(x₁)Q(x₂)Q(x₃).

------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluate the product Q(x₁)Q(x₂)Q(x₃)

Because x₁ ,x₂ ,x₃ are the roots of f(x), the product ∏Q(x_i) is the
resultant of f and Q:

 P = Res_x( f(x), Q(x) ).

Since f is monic, this resultant is simply the determinant of the 5×5
Sylvester matrix of f and Q.

Sylvester matrix (rows of f followed by rows of Q):

|  1  −1  −5  −1   0 |
|  0   1  −1  −5  −1 |
| −5   4  31   0   0 |
|  0  −5   4  31   0 |
|  0   0  −5   4  31 |

Row–operation reduction gives an upper-triangular matrix with diagonal
entries 1, 5, 4, 111/5, 31; hence

 Res = 1·5·4·(111/5)·31 = 4·111·31 = 444.

(Every row operation used was “add a multiple of another row”, which does
not alter the determinant.)

Thus

 P = Q(x₁)Q(x₂)Q(x₃) = 444.

------------------------------------------------
Final result

\boxed{444}

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

  x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.  Put  

 S₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ , S₂ = x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ , S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ .

From the given polynomial  

 S₁ = 1 , S₂ = –5 , S₃ = 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Factor each quadratic that appears

For any two numbers a , b

 a² – 4ab + b² = (a – k b)(a – k’ b)

where k = 2+√3 , k’ = 2–√3 (because k·k’ = 1 and k, k’ are the roots of t²–4t+1=0).

Hence  

P := ∏_{cyclic} (x_i² – 4x_i x_{i+1} + x_{i+1}²)  
  = ∏_{cyclic} (x_i – k x_{i+1}) (x_i – k’ x_{i+1})  
  = A(k)·A(k’),

where, for an indeterminate t,

 A(t) = (x₁ – t x₂)(x₂ – t x₃)(x₃ – t x₁).   (1)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Expand A(t)

Write  

A(t) = S₃ + B t + C t² – S₃ t³         (2)

with  

 B = –(x₁x₃² + x₂²x₃ + x₁²x₂), C = x₂x₃² + x₁²x₃ + x₁x₂².

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Two linear relations for B and C

•  Put t = 1 in (1):

A(1) = (x₁–x₂)(x₂–x₃)(x₃–x₁) = Δ  
  = B + C.                                (3)

(The Vandermonde Δ := (x₁–x₂)(x₂–x₃)(x₃–x₁).)

•  Put t = –1 in (1):

A(–1) = (x₁+x₂)(x₂+x₃)(x₃+x₁)  
       = S₁S₂ – S₃ = (1)(–5) – 1 = –6,    
but from (2)

A(–1) = 2S₃ – B + C = 2 – B + C.        (4)

Thus  

C – B = –8.                              (5)

Solving (3)–(5):

B = (Δ + 8)/2,  C = (Δ – 8)/2.         (6)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Evaluate A(k) and A(k’)

Because k satisfies k² = 4k – 1 and k³ = 15k – 4,

A(k) = S₃ + Bk + Ck² – S₃k³
      = 5S₃ – C + (B – 15S₃ + 4C)k.       (7)

Set  

P₁ = 5S₃ – C = 5 – C,    
P₂ = B – 15S₃ + 4C = B – 15 + 4C.

From (6) and S₃ = 1  

P₁ = (18 – Δ)/2,        P₂ = (5Δ – 54)/2.

Since k·k’ = 1, we have

A(k) = P₁ + P₂ k,       A(k’) = P₁ + P₂/k.

Hence  

P = A(k)A(k’) = (P₁ + P₂ k)(P₁ + P₂/k)
              = P₁² + (k + 1/k)P₁P₂ + P₂².   (8)

Because k + 1/k = 4,

P = P₁² + 4P₁P₂ + P₂²
  = (P₁ + P₂)² + 2P₁P₂.                   (9)

Compute

P₁ + P₂ = 2Δ – 18,         
P₁P₂   = ( –5Δ² + 144Δ – 972 )/4.

Insert into (9):

P = (2Δ – 18)² + 2·( –5Δ² + 144Δ – 972 )/4
  = 4Δ² – 72Δ + 324 + ( –5Δ² + 144Δ – 972 )/2
  = (3Δ² – 324)/2
  = 3(Δ² – 108)/2.                         (10)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Use the discriminant

For a monic cubic, the discriminant is Δ².  
For x³ – x² – 5x – 1 the discriminant is

Δ² = b²c² – 4c³ – 4b³d – 27d² + 18bcd
    = (1)(25) – 4(–125) – 4(–1)(–1) – 27(1) + 18(–1)(–5)(–1)
    = 25 + 500 – 4 – 27 – 90 = 404.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Final value

Insert Δ² = 404 into (10):

P = 3(404 – 108)/2
  = 3·296 / 2
  = 888 / 2
  = 444.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer

\boxed{444}

Let  

x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0 , roots  x₁ , x₂ , x₃    (1)

Set  

S₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ , S₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ , S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ .

From (1): S₁ = 1 , S₂ = –5 , S₃ = 1.    (2)

1.  A useful factorisation  
For every pair (i , j)

x_i² – 4x_i x_j + x_j² = (x_i – α x_j)(x_i – β x_j)          (3)

where α , β are the two roots of  t² – 4t + 1 = 0, i.e.  

α = 2 + √3 , β = 2 – √3 , α + β = 4 , αβ = 1.          (4)

2.  Transforming the whole product  
Write  

P = ∏_{i<j} (x_i² – 4x_i x_j + x_j²)
  = ∏_{i<j} (x_i – αx_j)(x_i – βx_j)        (by (3))
  = Q(α) Q(β)                                (5)

with  

Q(t) = (x₁ – t x₂)(x₂ – t x₃)(x₃ – t x₁)
     = S₃ – U t + V t² – S₃ t³,             (6)

where

U = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁,
V = x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁².                   (7)

The sums U and V can be expressed through the elementary
symmetric functions and the Vandermonde product

W = (x₁ – x₂)(x₂ – x₃)(x₃ – x₁).             (8)

Indeed

U + V = S₁S₂ – 3S₃ = –8                        (9)
U – V = –W.                                    (10)

3.  The discriminant
For the monic cubic (1) the discriminant is

Δ = 18S₁S₂S₃ – 4S₁³S₃ + S₁²S₂² – 4S₂³ – 27S₃²
   = 404.                                      (11)

But Δ = W², hence

W² = 404 , W = –2√101 (the sign is fixed by the order
                         x₁ > x₂ > x₃ from numerical inspection). (12)

Therefore

U = (–8 – W)/2 , V = (–8 + W)/2.              (13)

4.  Reducing Q(t) modulo t² – 4t + 1  
Divide Q(t) by g(t) = t² – 4t + 1 :

Q(t) = (–t + V – 4) g(t) + R(t),
R(t) = a t + b                                  (14)

with

a = –U + 4V – 15 , b = 5 – V.                  (15)

For the roots α, β of g(t) we have
Q(α) = R(α), Q(β) = R(β), hence

P = Q(α)Q(β) = (aα + b)(aβ + b)
             = a²(αβ) + ab(α + β) + b²
             = a² + 4ab + b².                  (16)

Introduce  

S = a + 2b = –U + 2V – 5 ,
T = b        = 5 – V.                          (17)

Then  
P = S² – 3T².                                  (18)

5.  Substituting U, V and W  
With (13):

S = (18 – 3W)/2 , T = (18 – W)/2.              (19)

Hence  

P = [(18 – 3W)/2]² – 3[(18 – W)/2]²
  = (3/2)(W² – 108).                           (20)

Using W² = 404 (11):

P = (3/2)(404 – 108) = (3/2)(296) = 444.       (21)

Therefore

(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) = 444.

Let the three distinct roots of

 xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ² = (xᵢ – a xⱼ)(xᵢ – b xⱼ),

where p_k = Σ xᵢ^k.

U – V = Δs₁ = Δ.

Thus  

U = –4 + Δ/2 , V = –4 – Δ/2, so U + V = –8.

4.  Compute P = Q(t)Q(t′) = Q(t)Q(1/t).

Using tt′ = 1 we find

P = –(t³ + t⁻³) + (U + V)(t² + t⁻²) – (U + V + UV)(t + t⁻¹) + (U² + V² + 2).

Because t + t⁻¹ = 4,
t² + t⁻² = (t + t⁻¹)² – 2 = 14,
t³ + t⁻³ = (t + t⁻¹)³ – 3(t + t⁻¹) = 52.

Substituting these and U + V = –8 gives

P = –52 + 14(–8) – 4(–8 + UV) + (U² + V² + 2)
  = –52 – 112 – 4UV + U² + V² + 2
  = –162 – 4UV + U² + V².

With U = –4 + Δ/2 , V = –4 – Δ/2 :

UV = 16 – Δ²/4, U² + V² = 32 + Δ²/2.

Hence

P = –162 – 4(16 – Δ²/4) + 32 + Δ²/2
  = –162 – 64 + Δ² + 32 + Δ²/2
  = –194 + (3/2)Δ².

5.  Evaluate Δ², the discriminant of the cubic.  
For x³ + ax² + bx + c,  

Δ² = a²b² – 4b³ – 4a³c – 27c² + 18abc.

Here a = –1, b = –5, c = –1:

Δ² = (–1)²(–5)² – 4(–5)³ – 4(–1)³(–1) – 27(–1)² + 18(–1)(–5)(–1)
    = 25 + 500 – 4 – 27 – 90
    = 404.

6.  Insert Δ² into P:

P = –194 + (3/2)(404)
  = –194 + 606
  = 444.

Therefore

\boxed{444}

Let  

S₁ = x₁ + x₂ + x₃ ,   
S₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ ,   
S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ .  

For the monic cubic  

x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0                

we have, by Viète’s relations,  

S₁ = 1 , S₂ = –5 , S₃ = 1 .

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Writing the required product as a symmetric polynomial
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Define  

A₁₂ = x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂² ,  
A₂₃ = x₂² – 4x₂x₃ + x₃² ,  
A₃₁ = x₃² – 4x₃x₁ + x₁² .

The quantity we need is  

P = A₁₂ A₂₃ A₃₁ ,

a symmetric polynomial of total degree 6 in x₁, x₂, x₃.  
Hence it can be expressed in the elementary symmetric functions as  

P = b S₁⁴S₂ + c S₁³S₃ + d S₁²S₂² + e S₁S₂S₃
    + f S₂³ + g S₃² ,               (1)

(the term S₁⁶ is absent because it will shortly be seen to have zero
coefficient).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Determining the coefficients b, c, d, e, f, g
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Choose convenient triples (x₁,x₂,x₃), compute P and the
corresponding S₁, S₂, S₃, and substitute into (1).

(1,0,0): P = 0 ⇒ coefficient of S₁⁶ is 0.                             

(1,1,0): P = –2 gives 16b + 4d + f = 4.                        (E₁)

(1,–1,0): P = 6 gives      f = –6.                         (E₂)

Hence from (E₁): 4b + d = 1.                                   (E₃)

(1,1,1): P = –8 → 243b + 27c + 81d + 9e + g = 154.        (E₄)

(1,2,3): P = –66 → 14256b + 1296c + 4356d + 396e + 36g = 7920. (E₅)

(1,–1,1): P = –72 → –b – c + d + e + g = –78.            (E₆)

(2,1,–1): P = –234 → –4b – 4c + d + e + g = –60.        (E₇)

Solving (E₂)–(E₇) step by step:

• From (E₂): f = –6.  
• From (E₃):  d = 1 – 4b.  
• From (E₆) & (E₇): b + c = –6 , e + g = –85 + 4b.  
• Substituting these plus d into (E₄) and (E₅) yields b = 0.  

Back–substitution then gives  
c = –6 , d = 1 , e = 40 , g = –125 .

(The set {b,c,d,e,f,g} is therefore {0, –6, 1, 40, –6, –125}.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluating P for the given cubic
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Insert S₁ = 1,  S₂ = –5,  S₃ = 1 into (1):

P = bS₁⁴S₂ + cS₁³S₃ + dS₁²S₂² + eS₁S₂S₃ + fS₂³ + gS₃²
  = 0·1⁴(–5) + (–6)·1³·1 + 1·1²·(–5)² + 40·1·(–5)·1
    + (–6)(–5)³ + (–125)·1²

  = 0 – 6 + 25 – 200 + 750 – 125
  = 444.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Result
--------------------------------------------------------------------
The required value is  

\boxed{444}.

Let  

f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1=0   with roots x₁,x₂,x₃  
e₁=x₁+x₂+x₃=1 ,  e₂=x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ =-5 ,  e₃=x₁x₂x₃=1.

1.  Rewrite each quadratic factor.

x_i^2-4x_i x_j+x_j^2=(x_i+x_j)^2-6x_i x_j.  
Since x_i+x_j=e₁-x_k and x_i x_j = e₃/x_k,  

x_i^2-4x_i x_j+x_j^2= (1-x_k)^2-\dfrac{6}{x_k}.

2.  Product to be found.

P=∏_{cyc}(x_i^2-4x_i x_j+x_j^2)
  =∏_{k=1}^{3}\Big[(1-x_k)^2-\dfrac{6}{x_k}\Big].

Multiply each factor by x_k (their product is x₁x₂x₃=e₃=1), giving  

P=∏_{k=1}^{3}g(x_k), where  
g(t)=t\Big[(1-t)^2-\dfrac{6}{t}\Big]=-t^{2}+6t-5.

Hence P=g(x₁)g(x₂)g(x₃).

3.  Use the resultant.

For polynomials  
f(t)=t^{3}-t^{2}-5t-1 and g(t)=-t^{2}+6t-5,

P equals the resultant Res(f,g) (leading coefficient of f is 1):

P = Res(f,g).

Because g(t)= - (t^{2}-6t+5)= -q(t),  

Res(f,g)=(-1)^{\deg f}\,Res(f,q)= -\,Res(f,q).

Compute Res(f,q) with the Sylvester determinant (size 5):

| 1  -1  -5  -1   0 |
| 0   1  -1  -5  -1 |
| 1  -6   5   0   0 |
| 0   1  -6   5   0 |
| 0   0   1  -6   5 | = -444.

Therefore  
P = -Res(f,q) = -(-444) = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let  

s₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ , s₂ = x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁ , s₃ = x₁x₂x₃  

For the cubic  

x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0  

Vieta’s relations give  

s₁ = 1 , s₂ = –5 , s₃ = 1.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Re-write each quadratic factor

xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ² = (xᵢ – αxⱼ)(xᵢ – βxⱼ)  

where α , β are the roots of t² – 4t + 1 = 0, i.e.  

α = 2+√3 , β = 2–√3 , α+β = 4 , αβ = 1.

Hence the required product is  

P = ∏₍cyc₎ (xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ²) = G(α)·G(β)  

with  

G(λ) := (x₁ – λx₂)(x₂ – λx₃)(x₃ – λx₁).
--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Expand G(λ)

G(λ) = 1 – C₁λ + D₁λ² – λ³, where  

C₁ = x₁x₃² + x₂²x₃ + x₁²x₂,  
D₁ = x₂x₃² + x₁²x₃ + x₁x₂².

Using power sums p₁ = s₁, p₂ = s₁² – 2s₂ = 11, p₃ = s₁p₂ – s₂p₁ + 3s₃ = 19,

C₁ + D₁ = s₁p₂ – p₃ = –8.                              (1)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Evaluate G at α and β

Because α² = 4α – 1 and α³ = 15α – 4 (the same for β),  

G(α) = (5 – D₁) + ( –C₁ + 4D₁ – 15 ) α  
      = A + Bα ,   with   A = 5 – D₁, B = –C₁ + 4D₁ – 15.

Thus  

P = G(α)G(β) = A² + 4AB + B².                          (2)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Express A and B through C₁ – D₁

Put S = C₁ + D₁ = –8 and d = C₁ – D₁.  
Then

A = 9 + d/2 , B = –27 – 5d/2.

Substituting into (2):

P = –162 + 3d²/2.                                      (3)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
5.  Relate d to the discriminant

Compute d:

d = C₁ – D₁ = (x₁ – x₂)(x₂ – x₃)(x₃ – x₁).

Hence d² is exactly the discriminant Δ of the cubic.

For ax³+bx²+cx+d the discriminant is  

Δ = b²c² – 4ac³ – 4b³d – 27a²d² + 18abcd.

With a=1, b= –1, c= –5, d= –1:

Δ = 25 + 500 – 4 – 27 – 90 = 404.                       (4)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
6.  Final computation

Insert d² = 404 into (3):

P = –162 + 3·404/2 = –162 + 606 = 444.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Final answer

\boxed{444}

Let the three (distinct) roots of  

 x³ − x² − 5x − 1 = 0  

be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
   S₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
   S₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = −5  
   S₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1

2.  Re–write the quadratic factor  

 x² − 4xy + y² = (x − αy)(x − βy) with  
 α = 2 + √3 , β = 2 − √3  (so α+β = 4 , αβ = 1).

3.  Define

 f(x,y) = x² − 4xy + y² = (x − αy)(x − βy).

   The required product is  

 P = f(x₁,x₂) f(x₂,x₃) f(x₃,x₁)  
   = Q(α) · Q(β),

   where  

 Q(t) = (x₁ − t x₂)(x₂ − t x₃)(x₃ − t x₁).

4.  Expand Q(t).  Writing a = x₁ , b = x₂ , c = x₃,

 Q(t) = abc − P₁ t + Q₁ t² − abc t³,

   with  

 P₁ = a²b + b²c + c²a, Q₁ = ab² + bc² + ca².

   Using identities

 P₁ + Q₁ = S₁S₂ − 3S₃ = 1(−5) − 3(1) = −8,  
 P₁ − Q₁ = (a−b)(b−c)(c−a),

   and the discriminant Δ of the cubic  

 Δ = (a−b)²(b−c)²(c−a)²  
    = A²B² − 4B³ − 4A³C − 27C² + 18ABC  
    (for x³ + Ax² + Bx + C),

   we have A = −1, B = −5, C = −1, giving Δ = 404.  
   Hence |P₁ − Q₁| = √Δ = 2√101, and taking the positive value

 P₁ = −4 + √101, Q₁ = −4 − √101.

   Therefore  

 Q(t) = 1 − (−4+√101)t + (−4−√101)t² − t³
      = 1 − P t + Q t² − t³,
   with P = −4 + √101, Q = −4 − √101.

5.  Compute R(u) = Q(u) Q(1/u), which is symmetric in u and 1/u.  
   Put y = u + 1/u; then

 R(u) = −y³ + (P+Q)y² + (3 − P − Q − PQ)y + (2 + P² + Q²).

   Since P+Q = −8, PQ = −85, P²+Q² = 234,

 R(u) = −y³ − 8y² + 96y + 252.

6.  With u = α and β = 1/α, we have y = α + β = 4.  
   Therefore

 P = Q(α) Q(β) = R(4)
   = −4³ − 8·4² + 96·4 + 252
   = −64 − 128 + 384 + 252
   = 444.

Hence  

(x₁² − 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)(x₂² − 4x₂x₃ + x₃²)(x₃² − 4x₃x₁ + x₁²) = 444.

Let the cubic be  
f(x)=x³−x²−5x−1=0 with distinct roots x₁,x₂,x₃.  
Denote  

e₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
e₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = –5  
e₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1  

(the elementary symmetric sums).

1.  Rewrite the required product  

P = (x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²)(x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃²)(x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁²).

Note that xᵢ²−4xᵢxⱼ+xⱼ² = (xᵢ−rxⱼ)(xᵢ−sxⱼ)  
where r,s are the two roots of t²−4t+1=0, namely  

r = 2+√3 ,   s = 2−√3 ,   rs = 1 ,   r+s = 4.

Hence  

P = ∏_{cyc}(xᵢ−rxⱼ) · ∏_{cyc}(xᵢ−sxⱼ) = F(r)F(s)         (1)

with  

F(t) = (x₁−tx₂)(x₂−tx₃)(x₃−tx₁)
     = 1 − b₁ t + c₁ t² − t³,

where  

b₁ = x₁²x₂ + x₂²x₃ + x₃²x₁,   
c₁ = x₁x₂² + x₂x₃² + x₃x₁².                              (2)

2.  Because r s = 1, (1) may be written as  

P = F(t)F(1/t)    with t = r.                              (3)

3.  Reduce F(t) modulo t²−4t+1 (which r and s satisfy):
t² = 4t−1 ,  t³ = 15t−4.

Thus  

F(t) = 1 − b₁t + c₁(4t−1) − (15t−4)
     = (5−c₁) + (−b₁+4c₁−15) t.                          (4)

The same reduction holds for F(1/t) with t replaced by 1/t ( = s ).
Let  

A = 5−c₁ ,   B = −b₁ + 4c₁ −15.                         (5)

Then, using r+s =4 and rs =1,

P = (A+Br)(A+Bs) = A² + 4AB + B².                       (6)

4.  Express A and B only with c₁.

From (2) one has  

(b₁ + c₁) = e₂ − 3e₃ = −5 −3 = −8.                     (7)

The discriminant of f(x),

Δ = (x₁−x₂)²(x₂−x₃)²(x₃−x₁)²
   = 404,

and F(1)= (x₁−x₂)(x₂−x₃)(x₃−x₁)= c₁−b₁,
so  

(c₁ − b₁)² = Δ ⇒ c₁ − b₁ = 2√101 (positive).           (8)

Solving (7) and (8):

c₁ = −4 + √101 ,       b₁ = −4 − √101.                 (9)

5.  Compute P.

First, find A and B with (5):

A = 5 − c₁ = 9 − √101,  
B = 5c₁ − 7 = 5(−4 + √101) − 7 = −27 + 5√101.

But only A² , AB , B² are needed; using (9) directly in (6) is simpler.

Put c₁ into (6).  With algebra,

P = 6(c₁² + 8c₁ − 11).

Now, using c₁ = −4 + √101:

c₁² + 8c₁ − 11 = (−4 + √101)² + 8(−4 + √101) − 11
               = (16 − 8√101 + 101) − 32 + 8√101 − 11
               = 85 − 11 = 74.

Therefore  

P = 6 · 74 = 444.

Hence

\boxed{444}

Let the roots of  
x³ – x² – 5x – 1 = 0 be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Elementary symmetric sums  
   s₁ = x₁+x₂+x₃ = 1  
   s₂ = x₁x₂ + x₂x₃ + x₃x₁ = –5  
   s₃ = x₁x₂x₃ = 1

2.  Write the required product in factored form  
   xᵢ² – 4xᵢxⱼ + xⱼ² = (xᵢ – αxⱼ)(xᵢ – βxⱼ)  
   where α = 2+√3,  β = 2–√3 (αβ = 1, α+β = 4).

   Therefore  
   P = ∏₍cyc₎(x₁² – 4x₁x₂ + x₂²)  
     = F(α) F(β) with  
   F(t) = (x₁ – t x₂)(x₂ – t x₃)(x₃ – t x₁).

3.  Expand F(t):  
   F(t) = s₃ + c₁t + c₂t² – s₃t³  
   with  
   c₁ = –∑ a²b , c₂ = ∑ ab²  
   Put D = (x₁–x₂)(x₂–x₃)(x₃–x₁).  
   Then  
   ∑ a²b = (s₁s₂ – 3s₃ + D)/2 , ∑ ab² = (s₁s₂ – 3s₃ – D)/2.

   Hence  
   F(t) = s₃ + C₁t + C₂t² – s₃t³  
   with  
   C₁ = –(s₁s₂ – 3s₃ + D)/2 ,  
   C₂ =  (s₁s₂ – 3s₃ – D)/2.

4.  Because α,β satisfy t² – 4t + 1 = 0, reduce t² and t³:

   t² = 4t – 1, t³ = 15t – 4.

   Thus F(t) (on a root of t²–4t+1) becomes linear:  
   F(t) = A + Bt, where  
   A = 5s₃ – C₂ = ( –s₁s₂ + 13s₃ + D )/2,  
   B = C₁ + 4C₂ – 15s₃ = ( 3s₁s₂ – 39s₃ – 5D )/2.

5.  Product at the two conjugate values:

   P = F(α)F(β) = A² + 4AB + B²
     = (A + 2B)² – 3B².

   Compute  
   A + 2B = (5s₁s₂ – 65s₃ – 9D)/2,  
   2B   = (3s₁s₂ – 39s₃ – 5D).

   After simplification  
   P = [ –(s₁s₂)² – 169s₃² + 26s₁s₂s₃ + 3D² ] / 2.

6.  Insert the known elementary sums (s₁=1, s₂=–5, s₃=1):

   –(s₁s₂)² = –(–5)² = –25  
   –169s₃²  = –169  
   26s₁s₂s₃ = 26(1)(–5)(1) = –130.

   The discriminant of the cubic is  
   Δ = (x₁–x₂)²(x₂–x₃)²(x₃–x₁)² = D²  
      = a²b² – 4b³ – 4a³c – 27c² + 18abc  
      (with a = –1, b = –5, c = –1)  
      = 25 + 500 – 4 – 27 – 90 = 404.

   Hence D² = 404 and 3D² = 1212.

   Numerator: –25 –169 –130 + 1212 = 888.  
   Therefore  
   P = 888 / 2 = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let the three distinct roots of  
f(x)=x^{3}-x^{2}-5x-1=0  
be x₁ , x₂ , x₃.

1.  Express each factor in the required product.

   x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²  
   = (x₁+x₂)²−6x₁x₂  

   Using the elementary symmetric sums  
   S₁=x₁+x₂+x₃=1 ,    
   S₂=x₁x₂+x₂x₃+x₃x₁=−5 ,

   and writing x₃ = the root not appearing in this factor, we have  
   x₁+x₂ = S₁−x₃,     
   x₁x₂ = S₂−x₃S₁+x₃².

   Hence  

   x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂² = S₁²−6S₂+4S₁x₃−5x₃².  

   Because S₁=1 and S₂=−5, the constant part becomes  
   S₁²−6S₂ = 1+30 = 31.

   Therefore, defining g(t)=31+4t−5t², we get      

   x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂² = g(x₃),  
   x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃² = g(x₁),  
   x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁² = g(x₂).

2.  The desired product is thus  

   P = g(x₁)g(x₂)g(x₃).

3.  Computing the product without finding the roots.

   For any monic polynomial f of degree n and any polynomial g,  
   the product ∏_{i=1}^{n} g(r_i) over the roots r_i of f equals the
   resultant Res(f,g).

   Here f is monic of degree 3, g has degree 2, so  

   P = Res(f,g),  where  
   f(x)=x³−x²−5x−1,  g(x)=−5x²+4x+31.

4.  Determinant of the Sylvester matrix.

   The 5×5 Sylvester matrix of f and g (rows of shifted coefficients)
   is  

   | 1  −1  −5  −1   0 |  
   | 0   1  −1  −5  −1 |  
   |−5   4  31   0   0 |  
   | 0  −5   4  31   0 |  
   | 0   0  −5   4  31 |

   Row-operations preserving the determinant give

   → determinant = det  

   | 1  −1  −5  −1   0 |  
   | 0   1  −1  −5  −1 |  
   | 0  −1   6  −5   0 |  
   | 0  −5   4  31   0 |  
   | 0   0  −5   4  31 |

   Expanding along the first column reduces the computation to the
   determinant of the 4×4 sub-matrix

   | 1  −1  −5  −1 |  
   |−1   6  −5   0 |  
   |−5   4  31   0 |  
   | 0  −5   4  31 |

   Further elimination and a 3×3 expansion yield

   det = 444.

5.  Therefore

   (x₁²−4x₁x₂+x₂²)
   (x₂²−4x₂x₃+x₃²)
   (x₃²−4x₃x₁+x₁²) = 444.

\boxed{444}

Let the six faces of the die be denoted by the set  
S = {1,2,3,4,5,6}.  
For the six rolls, let  
F₁,F₂,…,F₆ ∈ S  
be the faces that show on top (and hence receive stickers 1,…,6).  
A face is blank at the end iff it never appears among the Fᵢ.

Event B: “all even–numbered stickers 2,4,6 are visible”  
This is equivalent to  
  • F₂ is different from every one of F₃,F₄,F₅,F₆, and  
  • F₄ is different from F₅ and F₆.  

Event A: “exactly one face is blank”  
⇔ the six rolls use exactly five distinct faces.

-------------------------------------------------
1.  Probability of B

Independence of the rolls gives

P(F₂ ≠ F₃,F₄,F₅,F₆) = (5/6)⁴ = 625/1296.

Given F₂, the later rolls cannot equal F₂, so F₄,F₅,F₆ are i.i.d. among 5 faces.  
Hence

P(F₄ ≠ F₅,F₆ | F₂ condition) = (4/5)² = 16/25.

Therefore  
P(B) = (625/1296)·(16/25) = 25/81.

-------------------------------------------------
2.  Probability of A and B

Fix one specific blank face b (6 choices).

The remaining 5 faces R = S \{b}.  
Choose the three distinct faces for stickers 2,4,6:

number of ordered triples (F₂,F₄,F₆) with distinct entries in R  
= 5·4·3 = 60.

Write
U = {F₂,F₄,F₆};   the two still–unused faces in R are X,Y.

Now determine (F₁,F₃,F₅) subject to

   • F₁,F₃,F₅ ∈ R (never the blank b),  
   • F₃ ≠ F₂,  
   • F₅ ≠ F₂ and F₅ ≠ F₄,  
   • together with U they must use both X and Y (so that all five faces in R are visited).

Without the “use X and Y” requirement there are  
5·4·3 = 60 ordered triples (F₁,F₃,F₅):

 F₁ : 5 options in R  
 F₃ : 4 options in R\{F₂}  
 F₅ : 3 options in R\{F₂,F₄}.

Count those that fail to include X or Y.

Missing X: 4·3·2 = 24  
Missing Y: 24  
Missing both X and Y: 3·2·1 = 6.

By inclusion–exclusion, triples that include both X and Y:

60 – 24 – 24 + 6 = 18.

Hence, for each fixed (b, F₂,F₄,F₆), there are 18 good choices of (F₁,F₃,F₅).

Total sequences with A ∧ B:

6 (choices of b) · 60 (choices of F₂,F₄,F₆) · 18 (choices of F₁,F₃,F₅)  
= 6480.

Probability:

P(A ∧ B) = 6480 / 6⁶ = 6480 / 46656 = 5/36.

-------------------------------------------------
3.  Conditional probability

p = P(A | B) = P(A ∧ B) / P(B)  
  = (5/36) / (25/81) = (5/36)·(81/25) = 9/20.

Thus p = 9/20 = m/n with m+n = 9+20 = 29.

• f₅ : any face ≠ f₂,f₄, 4 ways.

Event B: exactly one face of the die is never rolled (remains blank).

Let R be the three remaining faces.

f₁ : any of the 5 faces a,b,c,r₁,r₂               (5 choices)  
f₃ : any of those 5 except a                     (4 choices)  
f₅ : any of those 5 except a,b                   (3 choices)

• Omit r₁ : 4·3·2 = 24  
• Omit r₂ : 24

\(F_1:\;6\) choices  
\(F_2:\;6\) choices  
\(F_3:\;5\) choices (not \(F_2\))  
\(F_4:\;5\) choices (not \(F_2\))  
\(F_5:\;4\) choices (not \(F_2,F_4\))  
\(F_6:\;4\) choices (not \(F_2,F_4\))

• Triples missing \(B\): \(24\)

\(N_{U}= 60\,(F_2,F_4,F_6)\times 18\,(F_1,F_3,F_5)=1080.\)

Let the face that a sticker lands on during its roll be denoted by  
f(1),f(2),…,f(6)∈{1,2,3,4,5,6}.  
The six rolls are independent and each f(k) is uniformly distributed on {1,…,6}.

After every roll the sticker that was just applied is on top of anything already on that face, so the sticker that remains visible on a face is the one with the greatest label that ever landed there.

Definitions  
A : exactly one face is blank at the end (so the six stickers occupy exactly 5 faces).  
B : stickers 2,4 and 6 are visible.

The required probability is  

p = P(A∧B) / P(B).

------------------------------------------------
1.  Probability that 2, 4 and 6 are visible  

• 2 is visible iff none of the stickers 3–6 land on f(2).  
  Probability = (5/6)^4.  

• Conditional on that, 4 is visible iff neither 5 nor 6 land on f(4) and, of course, f(4)≠f(2).  
  Probability = (5/6)·(4/6)·(4/6).

Multiplying gives  

P(B)= (5/6)^2 · (4/6)^2 = 25·16 / 6^4 = 25/81.                 (1)

------------------------------------------------
2.  Counting favourable assignments for A ∧ B  

Event B forbids the following equalities of faces  

f(2)=f(3), f(2)=f(4), f(2)=f(5), f(2)=f(6),  
f(4)=f(5), f(4)=f(6).                         (six forbidden pairs)

Thus the pairs that are allowed to coincide are exactly the 15−6 = 9 pairs

{1,2}, {1,3}, {1,4}, {1,5}, {1,6},  
{3,4}, {3,5}, {3,6}, {5,6}.

For A we must have exactly one pair of equal faces and all other four stickers on different faces, none of them equal to the duplicate face.  
Choose

• which of the 9 admissible pairs is the duplicate,  
• the common face for that pair (6 choices),  
• 4 distinct faces for the remaining 4 stickers chosen from the remaining 5 faces and assigned to the 4 labels (5P4 = 5·4·3·2 = 120 ways).

Hence the number of assignments with A ∧ B is  

9 · 6 · 120 = 6480.

Total number of possible assignments is 6^6 = 46656, so  

P(A ∧ B)= 6480 / 46656 = 5/36.                          (2)

------------------------------------------------
3.  The conditional probability  

p = (5/36) ÷ (25/81) = (5/36) · (81/25) = 9/20.

------------------------------------------------
4.  Result  

p = 9/20 = m/n with m=9, n=20, therefore  

m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

Let  

F1 , F2 , … , F6    be the faces that come up on the six rolls.  
Sticker k is put on face Fk (covering anything already there).

A face is blank at the end iff it never appears among the six Fk’s.

Event E (given condition).  
All the even–numbered stickers (2,4,6) are visible.  
That is equivalent to the following conditions:

1. F2 , F4 , F6 are three distinct faces.  
2. After roll 2 the die never again shows F2, so  
 F3 , F4 , F5 , F6 ≠ F2.  
3. After roll 4 the die never again shows F4, so  
 F5 , F6 ≠ F4.

There is no restriction on F1, and no further restriction on F6.

Counting sequences that satisfy E  
(Each of the 6^6 sequences of faces is equally likely.)

• Choose the ordered triple (F2 , F4 , F6): 6·5·4 = 120 ways  
 (distinct faces).  
• F1: 6 options.  
• F3: any face ≠ F2 → 5 options.  
• F5: any face ≠ F2 , F4 → 4 options.

Hence  
NE = 120 · 6 · 5 · 4 = 14 400.

Event A.  
Exactly one face is blank ⇒ exactly 5 different faces appear, i.e.  
|{F1,…,F6}| = 5.

We now count sequences that satisfy both A and E.

Fix the ordered triple (F2 = a , F4 = b , F6 = c) (still 120 possibilities).  
Let S = {a, b, c}; |S| = 3.

Among the remaining three rolls (F1, F3, F5)

• F3 ≠ a, F5 ≠ a, b (from E)  
• altogether they must introduce exactly two new faces (so that the total number of distinct
  faces becomes 5) and may repeat one already used face.

Write T ⊂ {faces}\S for those two new faces; |T| = 2.  
There are C(3,2) = 3 choices for T.  
Let the remaining unused face be d (the blank one).

The ordered triple (F1, F3, F5) must

– use only faces in S ∪ T (never d)  
– include both faces of T at least once  
– respect F3 ≠ a and F5 ≠ a, b.

With the symbols

x = F1 (5 options: S ∪ T)  
y = F3 (4 options: {b, c} ∪ T)  (y ≠ a)  
z = F5 (3 options: {c} ∪ T)   (z ≠ a, b)

there are 5·4·3 = 60 ordered triples (x,y,z) before imposing the “contain both T-faces” requirement.

Count those 60 triples that contain both members of T:

Let Ap (resp. Aq) be the set of triples that omit one of the two T-faces p or q.

#(omit p) = 4·3·2 = 24  
#(omit q) = 24 (symmetric)  
#(omit both p and q) = 3·2·1 = 6

Hence  
#(contain both p and q) = 60 − 24 − 24 + 6 = 18.

So, for each fixed (a,b,c) there are

#(T)·18 = 3·18 = 54

valid choices of (F1, F3, F5).

Therefore  
N_AE = 120 · 54 = 6 480.

Conditional probability

p = P(A | E) = N_AE / N_E = 6 480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

Thus m = 9, n = 20 and m + n = 29.

Let the six rolls of the fair die give the ordered sequence of top faces  

(F₁, F₂, F₃, F₄, F₅, F₆).

Sticker i is placed on face Fᵢ, covering any sticker already there.  
The sticker visible on a face is therefore the one with the largest index that
ever landed on that face.  
A face is blank at the end iff it never appears among the six rolls.

1.  “Even stickers visible’’ conditions  
   • F₂, F₄, F₆ must be on three different faces.  
   • Nothing rolled after roll 2 may equal F₂.  
   • Nothing rolled after roll 4 may equal F₄.

   Thus the constraints are

   F₃≠F₂, F₄≠F₂, F₅≠F₂,F₄, F₆≠F₂,F₄, and F₂,F₄,F₆ all distinct.

2.  Counting all sequences satisfying these constraints  

   For the 6 rolls choose consecutively:

   F₁: 6 choices  
   F₂: 6 choices  
   F₃: 5 choices (≠F₂)  
   F₄: 5 choices (≠F₂)  
   F₅: 4 choices (≠F₂,F₄)  
   F₆: 4 choices (≠F₂,F₄)

   N(E)=6·6·5·5·4·4=14 400.

3.  A convenient formula  

   If k specific faces are forbidden, r=6–k faces remain usable.
   Provided r≥3 (otherwise no sequence is possible),

        g(k)=r²(r–1)²(r–2)²

   counts the sequences that avoid those k faces and still satisfy the
   constraints.

   Values needed:

        g(0)=6²·5²·4²=14 400  
        g(1)=5²·4²·3²=3 600  
        g(2)=4²·3²·2²=  576  
        g(3)=3²·2²·1²=   36  
        g(k)=0 for k≥4.

4.  Sequences missing faces  

   Let A_i be the event “face i never appears”.

   • At least one face missing  
     U₁ = Σ g(1) – Σ g(2) + Σ g(3)

           = C(6,1)g(1) – C(6,2)g(2) + C(6,3)g(3)
           = 6·3600 – 15·576 + 20·36
           = 21 600 – 8 640 + 720
           = 13 680.

   • At least two faces missing  
     U₂ = Σ g(2) – Σ g(3)
         = 8 640 – 720
         = 7 920.

   • Exactly one face missing (i.e. exactly five faces used)

        N₅ = U₁ – U₂ = 13 680 – 7 920 = 5 760.

5.  Conditional probability

        p = P(exactly one blank face | even stickers visible)
          = N₅ / N(E)
          = 5 760 / 14 400
          = 2/5.

Therefore, p = 2/5 = m/n with m+n = 2+5 = 7.

record which face of the die is on top when sticker 1,2,…,6 is placed.

   • f₄ : 5 choices (≠ f₂)  
   • f₅ : 4 choices (≠ f₂ , f₄)  
   • f₆ : 4 choices (≠ f₂ , f₄)

   N_A = 6·6·5·5·4·4 = 14 400

2.  Condition B (exactly one blank face)

   ⇒ the six rolls use exactly 5 distinct faces: one face appears twice, the other four appear once.

3.  Conditional probability

   p = P(B | A) = N_{A∩B} / N_A = 6 480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20

• X₅ : 4 choices (≠ X₂ , X₄)  
• X₆ : 4 choices (≠ X₂ , X₄)

Such a sequence must contain exactly one repeated face (a single duplicate pair) and must still obey the restrictions of E.

Total (A∩E) = 720 + 1 440 + 4 320 = 6 480.

3.  Conditional probability  

p = P(A | E) = |A∩E| / |E| = 6 480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

Label the six rolls of the die by  
T₁ ,T₂ ,T₃ ,T₄ ,T₅ ,T₆ , where Tᵢ is the upper face that appears on the i-th roll and immediately receives sticker i.

“Even stickers visible’’ means that the stickers 2,4,6 are on top of their faces at the end, i.e.

1. T₂ ≠ T₃ ,T₄ ,T₅ ,T₆ (face of 2 is never rolled again)  
2. T₄ ≠ T₅ ,T₆     (face of 4 is never rolled again)  
3. T₆ ≠ T₂ ,T₄     (sticker 6 must not cover 2 or 4).

There are no other requirements.  
Choosing the faces successively one obtains

 T₁ : 6 possibilities  
 T₂ : 6 possibilities  
 T₃ : 5 possibilities (must differ from T₂)  
 T₄ : 5 possibilities (must differ from T₂)  
 T₅ : 4 possibilities (must differ from T₂ and T₄)  
 T₆ : 4 possibilities (must differ from T₂ and T₄)

Hence the total number of equally likely sequences satisfying the visibility condition is

N_E = 6·6·5·5·4·4 = 14 400.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Counting sequences that leave exactly one face blank
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Call A_f the event that the face f never appears among the six rolls.  
For |S| = s let N_S be the number of sequences in which every face in S is absent.  
Because only the 6-s remaining faces can be used, the same “availability counts’’ as above but with 6 replaced by r = 6-s give

N_S = N(6-s) with    
N(r) = r·r·(r-1)·(r-1)·(r-2)·(r-2).

Thus  

N(6) = 14 400   (as above)  
N(5) = 5·5·4·4·3·3 = 3 600  
N(4) = 4·4·3·3·2·2 = 576  
N(3) = 3·3·2·2·1·1 = 36  
N(2) = 0       (r-2 would be 0).

Let a_k be the number of sequences that are missing exactly k faces (i.e. have 6-k distinct faces showing).  
The relations

A_s = Σ_{|S|=s} N_S = C(6,s) N(6-s) = Σ_{k=s}^6 C(k,s) a_k  ( s = 1,2,3 )

give a triangular system which is solved successively from s = 3 downwards:

1. s = 3 : A₃ = C(6,3)N(3) = 20·36 = 720  
  A₃ = C(3,3)a₃ ⇒ a₃ = 720.

2. s = 2 : A₂ = C(6,2)N(4) = 15·576 = 8 640  
  A₂ = a₂ + C(3,2)a₃  
  8 640 = a₂ + 3·720 ⇒ a₂ = 6 480.

3. s = 1 : A₁ = C(6,1)N(5) = 6·3 600 = 21 600  
  A₁ = a₁ + 2a₂ + 3a₃  
  21 600 = a₁ + 2·6 480 + 3·720  
  a₁ = 6 480.

So

• a₁ = 6 480 sequences leave exactly one face blank,  
• total sequences satisfying the even-visibility condition: N_E = 14 400.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conditional probability
--------------------------------------------------------------------
p = a₁ / N_E = 6 480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Answer
--------------------------------------------------------------------
m + n = 9 + 20 = 29

\boxed{29}

Step 1.  Model the six rolls  
Let r₁,r₂,…,r₆ be the faces that land on top in the six successive rolls.  
Each r_k is independent and uniformly distributed over the six faces.

Step 2.  Translate the condition “2,4,6 are visible”  
Sticker k is visible exactly when its face is never rolled again after the k-th roll.  
Therefore  

1. r₂, r₄ and r₆ must be three distinct faces (otherwise one would cover another).  
2. r₂ cannot appear among r₃,r₄,r₅,r₆.  
3. r₄ cannot appear among r₅,r₆.  

No other restrictions arise.  
These conditions define event E.

Step 3.  Count the sequences in E  
Choose ordered triple (r₂,r₄,r₆): 6·5·4 = 120 ways.  
Given (r₂,r₄,r₆)= (a,b,c):

• r₃ may be any face except a  → 5 choices.  
• r₅ may be any face except a or b → 4 choices.  
• r₁ is unrestricted → 6 choices.

So sequences in E per (a,b,c): 5·4·6 = 120.  
Total N_E = 120·120 = 14 400.

Step 4.  Impose “exactly one face left blank”  
A blank face is one never rolled.  Having exactly one blank means the set of faces  
{r₁,…,r₆} contains exactly 5 distinct faces.

With A = {a,b,c} = {r₂,r₄,r₆}, let B = {x,y,z} be the other three faces.  
Exactly one of B must stay blank; call it w.  The remaining two faces s,t ∈ B must each appear at least once among r₁,r₃,r₅, while w appears none.

Fix (a,b,c) and choice of blank w (3 ways).

Allowed choices (w excluded and other roll-specific restrictions):

r₁ ∈ {a,b,c,s,t}       – 5 options  
r₃ ∈ {b,c,s,t}         – 4 options (cannot be a)  
r₅ ∈ {c,s,t}           – 3 options (cannot be a or b)

Total sequences satisfying roll restrictions and excluding w: 5·4·3 = 60.

Count how many of those 60 sequences actually contain both s and t:

By inclusion–exclusion  
• sequences missing s: 4·3·2 = 24  
• sequences missing t: 24  
• sequences missing both s and t: 3·2·1 = 6  
Hence sequences containing both s and t: 60 − 24 − 24 + 6 = 18.

Thus, per (a,b,c) and blank w, there are 18 valid sequences.

Step 5.  Total sequences with E and exactly one blank  
Choices:

• (r₂,r₄,r₆): 120  
• blank face w: 3  
• sequences for (r₁,r₃,r₅): 18  

N_{B∩E} = 120·3·18 = 6 480.

Step 6.  Conditional probability  
p = P(B | E) = N_{B∩E} / N_E = 6 480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

Step 7.  Answer  
m = 9, n = 20, so m + n = 29.

\boxed{29}

Let the six stickers be placed on faces \(S_1,S_2,\dots ,S_6\) in that order (so \(S_k\) is the face that received sticker \(k\)).  
The rules “sticker \(2\) is visible’’ and “sticker \(4\) is visible’’ translate into

1. \(S_2\neq S_3,S_4,S_5,S_6\)   (sticker 2’s face is not reused later),  
2. \(S_4\neq S_5,S_6\)           (sticker 4’s face is not reused by 5 or 6).

Sticker 6 is always visible automatically.  
Call these conditions together event \(C\).

Total number of equally–likely sequences: \(6^6=46656\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Count of sequences satisfying \(C\).

Choose the faces successively, obeying the restrictions.

• \(S_1\): \(6\) choices.  
• \(S_2\): \(6\) choices.  
• \(S_3\): any face but \(S_2\): \(5\) choices.  
• \(S_4\): any face but \(S_2\): \(5\) choices.  
• \(S_5\): any face but \(S_2,S_4\): \(4\) choices.  
• \(S_6\): any face but \(S_2,S_4\): \(4\) choices.

Number of sequences with \(C\):
\(6\cdot6\cdot5\cdot5\cdot4\cdot4 = 14400\).

So \(\Pr(C)=\dfrac{14400}{46656}= \dfrac{25}{81}\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Add the requirement that exactly one face is blank.

Using six stickers, having exactly one blank face means exactly five distinct faces are used; in other words one face is used twice and the other four faces once each.

To count sequences satisfying both \(C\) and this “5–face’’ condition:

• Pick the unordered pair of stickers that share the repeated face.  
  Only 9 of the \( \binom{6}{2}=15\) pairs are allowed, because the pair must not violate \(C\):
     {2,3},{2,4},{2,5},{2,6},{4,5},{4,6} are forbidden.
  Hence 9 admissible pairs.

• Choose the repeated face \(R\): 6 possibilities.

• Assign \(R\) to those two stickers (fixed once \(R\) is chosen).

• The remaining four stickers must go to four distinct faces among the remaining five faces.
  This is an injection of four ordered stickers into five faces:
  \(5\cdot4\cdot3\cdot2 = 120\) possibilities.

Thus sequences with both properties:
\(9\;\text{pairs}\times 6\;\text{faces}\times 120 = 6480\).

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Desired conditional probability.

\[
p=\Pr(\text{exactly one blank}\mid C)=
\frac{6480}{14400}= \frac{9}{20}.
\]

--------------------------------------------------------------------
4.  Final result.

\(p=\dfrac{9}{20}\) is already in lowest terms, so \(m+n=9+20=29\).

Let  
X₁ , X₂ , … , X₆ ∈ {1,…,6} be the faces that appear on the top of the die on rolls 1 through 6.  
A face that is ever rolled receives a sticker whose number is the largest label that ever lands on it.

Event E (all even–numbered stickers visible):
1. Sticker 2 is visible  ⇔  no later roll lands on face X₂  
  ⇒ X₂ ≠ X₃ , X₄ , X₅ , X₆
2. Sticker 4 is visible  ⇔  no later roll lands on face X₄  
  ⇒ X₄ ≠ X₅ , X₆
3. Sticker 2 cannot be covered by sticker 4  
  ⇒ X₂ ≠ X₄  

There are otherwise no restrictions.  
Counting sequences that satisfy these conditions:

X₁ : 6 choices  
X₂ : 6 choices  
X₃ : 5 choices  (≠ X₂)  
X₄ : 5 choices  (≠ X₂)  
X₅ : 4 choices  (≠ X₂ , X₄)  
X₆ : 4 choices  (≠ X₂ , X₄)

N(E) = 6·6·5·5·4·4 = 14 400.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Sequences in E with exactly 5 distinct faces (event B)

Exactly one face must be repeated; all others appear once.  
Because of the visibility restrictions, the only pairs of indices that are
allowed to be equal are

(1,2), (1,3), (1,4), (1,5), (1,6), (3,4), (3,5), (3,6), (5,6)  (9 pairs)

Fix one such pair and let D be the common face for that pair.
The remaining four indices must be assigned four distinct faces different from D;
this guarantees the repetition is unique and keeps all restrictions intact.

For any of the 9 pairs:

choose D : 6 ways  
choose the four remaining faces, in order : 5·4·3·2 = 120 ways  

Hence each pair contributes 6·120 = 720 sequences, and

N(E ∩ B) = 9·720 = 6480.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
Conditional probability

p = P(B | E) = N(E ∩ B) / N(E) = 6480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
m + n = 9 + 20 = 29

Let the sequence of faces that appear on top in the six rolls be  

(X₁ , X₂ , X₃ , X₄ , X₅ , X₆).

After roll k the sticker k is put on the face Xk, covering anything that was already there.

1.  Condition E (all even stickers visible)

   • Let  
     a = X₂ , b = X₄ , c = X₆ .  
     The stickers 2, 4, 6 will be visible exactly when

       – a, b, c are three different faces,  
       – X₃ , X₄ , X₅ , X₆ are not a,  
       – X₅ , X₆ are not b,  
       – X₆ is not a or b.

     Equivalently  
       a , b , c distinct,  
       X₃ ≠ a ,  
       X₅ ≠ a , b ,  
       (no further restrictions on X₁).

   • Choices:

       a : 6 ways  
       b : 5 ways  
       c : 4 ways  
       X₃ : 5 ways (any but a)  
       X₅ : 4 ways (any but a , b)  
       X₁ : 6 ways (any face)

     Total sequences satisfying E:

       6·5·4·5·4·6 = 14 400.

2.  Condition B (exactly one blank face)

   There must be exactly 5 distinct faces among the 6 rolls, i.e. exactly one pair of positions shows the same face and all other positions are distinct.

   Fix a, b, c (distinct).  
   The remaining three faces constitute the set R (|R| = 3).

   Exactly one duplicate pair can happen in the following nine ways:

     1) (X₁, a) 2) (X₁, b) 3) (X₃, b)  
     4) (X₁, c) 5) (X₃, c) 6) (X₅, c)  
     7) (X₁, X₃) 8) (X₁, X₅) 9) (X₃, X₅),

   with the obvious restrictions that no other two positions duplicate.

   Example: for case 1 (X₁ = a)

       X₃ may be any of the 3 faces in R,  
       X₅ any of the remaining 2 faces of R,

       giving 3·2 = 6 sequences.

   Each of the nine cases gives precisely 6 sequences, so for fixed (a, b, c)

       N_{B∧E}(a,b,c) = 9·6 = 54.

   Since there are 6·5·4 = 120 possible (a, b, c),

       total N_{B∧E} = 120·54 = 6 480.

3.  Conditional probability

       p = P(B | E) = N_{B∧E} / N_E
         = 6 480 / 14 400
         = 54 / 120
         = 9 / 20.

Thus p = 9/20, so m = 9 and n = 20, and

m + n = 29.

Let  

r₁,r₂,…,r₆ = the faces that appear on the six consecutive rolls  
(the sticker numbered i is put on face rᵢ).

Total number of sequences of rolls: 6⁶.

Event E ( “2,4,6 are the visible stickers” )

1.  The faces of 2,4,6 are distinct:  r₂,r₄,r₆ all different.  
2.  After 2 is placed its face is never rolled again:  r₃,r₄,r₅,r₆ ≠ r₂.  
3.  After 4 is placed its face is never rolled again:  r₅,r₆ ≠ r₄.

That is the whole set of restrictions.

Counting sequences that satisfy E  

• Choose faces for r₂,r₄,r₆:  6·5·4 = 120  
• Choose r₁ freely:                               6  
• Choose r₃ (must avoid r₂):                     5  
• Choose r₅ (must avoid r₂,r₄):                  4  

count(E) = 120·6·5·4 = 14 400.

-------------------------------------------------------------------
Event B ( “exactly one face is left blank” )

Among the six rolls exactly five distinct faces appear, i.e.  
one face appears twice and the other four appear once.

Fix the already–chosen distinct faces  
f₂ = r₂ , f₄ = r₄ , f₆ = r₆ .

Let A be the other three faces of the die (|A| = 3).

We classify by which face is duplicated.

Case 1.  Duplicate is f₂  
 r₁ = f₂;   r₃,r₅ are two different faces from A.  
 Number of triples (r₁,r₃,r₅): 1 · (3·2) = 6.

Case 2.  Duplicate is f₄  
 Exactly one of r₁,r₃ equals f₄, the other two positions get two different faces from A.  
 2 choices for the position of the duplicate, 3·2 ways for the other two faces:  
 2 · 6 = 12.

Case 3.  Duplicate is f₆  
 Exactly one of r₁,r₃,r₅ equals f₆, the other two positions are different faces from A.  
 3 choices for the position, 3·2 ways for the other two faces:  
 3 · 6 = 18.

Case 4.  Duplicate is a new face G∈A  
 Pick G (3 ways) and another new face H (2 ways).  
 Choose the two positions for G among {r₁,r₃,r₅} (C(3,2)=3).  
 Total: 3·2·3 = 18.

Total triples (r₁,r₃,r₅) that realize B:  
6 + 12 + 18 + 18 = 54.

Hence for each fixed (f₂,f₄,f₆) there are 54 satisfactory triples, while without B there are 6·5·4 = 120 triples.  Therefore

P(B | E) = 54 / 120 = 9 / 20.

So p = 9/20 = m/n with m = 9, n = 20, and

m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

        # (B ∩ C) sequences = 6·5·4·54 = 6480.

4.  Conditional probability
