Let the ordered 6-tuple  
(F₁,F₂,F₃,F₄,F₅,F₆) record the face that is on top after each roll.  
Sticker t is placed at time t on face Fₜ.

A sticker numbered t is visible at the end iff no later roll lands on the same face.  
Thus, for the even stickers to be visible we must have

1. F₂ , F₄ , F₆ all different (otherwise one covers another);
2. F₃ ≠ F₂  (so 3 doesn’t cover 2);
3. F₅ ≠ F₂ , F₅ ≠ F₄ (so 5 doesn’t cover 2 or 4).

No other restrictions apply.

Counting sequences that make all three even stickers visible  
(event E):

• Choose the three distinct faces a=F₂ , b=F₄ , c=F₆ : 6·5·4 = 120 ways.  
• Choose F₃ (≠ a) : 5 ways.  
• Choose F₅ (≠ a,b) : 4 ways.  
• Choose F₁ freely : 6 ways.

|E| = 120·5·4·6 = 14 400.

Now add the requirement that exactly one face is still blank at the end (event B).  
That means exactly five distinct faces appear among F₁,…,F₆.

The three faces a,b,c are already distinct; we need exactly two more, call them d,e, selected from the remaining three faces.  
Choose them: C(3,2)=3 ways.

From the remaining ordered triple (F₁,F₃,F₅) = (x,y,z) we require

• y ≠ a, z ≠ a,b (as before);  
• x,y,z use only the faces a,b,c,d,e (never the unused face f);  
• both d and e appear at least once among x,y,z.

Allowed choices:

x: 5 possibilities {a,b,c,d,e}  
y: 4 possibilities {b,c,d,e}  
z: 3 possibilities {c,d,e}

Total without the “contain d and e” condition: 5·4·3 = 60.

Subtract those missing at least one of d or e.

Without d: x 4, y 3, z 2  → 24 sequences  
Without e: x 4, y 3, z 2  → 24 sequences  
Without both d and e: x 3, y 2, z 1 → 6 sequences.

Hence sequences that include both d and e: 60 – 24 – 24 + 6 = 18.

Thus

|B ∩ E| = (ways to choose a,b,c) · (ways to choose d,e) · (valid x,y,z)  
         = 120 · 3 · 18 = 6 480.

Conditional probability

p = |B ∩ E| / |E| = 6 480 / 14 400 = 648 / 1 440 = 9 / 20.

Therefore m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

• Choose R₃ among the same 5 faces.  
• Choose R₅ among the 4 faces ≠ R₂,R₄.  
• Choose R₆ among the 4 faces ≠ R₂,R₄.

Fix any one of these nine pairs and let the repeated face be X.

Having 6 rolls and 6 faces, “exactly one blank face’’ means that

Step 1.  Model the procedure  
Each of the six stickers is placed on the face that happens to be on top when that sticker is rolled.  
Thus the six faces chosen for stickers 1,2,3,4,5,6 are independent and uniformly random from the set  
{1,2,3,4,5,6}.  Let Fk be the face that receives sticker k.

After every sticker is placed the face shows the largest-numbered sticker that ever landed there.  
A face is blank iff no sticker ever lands on it.

Step 2.  Translate “all even stickers are visible”  
For sticker k to be visible, no later sticker may land on its face.

All even stickers visible  ⇔  
(a) F2 , F4 , F6 are three different faces;  
(b) F3 ≠ F2  (otherwise it would cover 2);  
(c) F5 ≠ F2 , F5 ≠ F4  (otherwise it would cover 2 or 4);  
(d) F6 is already distinct from F2 , F4 by (a).

Call the event that these four conditions hold E.

Counting E:  
F2 : 6 choices  
F4 : 5 (≠F2)  
F6 : 4 (≠F2 ,F4)  
F3 : 5 (≠F2)  
F5 : 4 (≠F2 ,F4)  
F1 : 6 (no restriction)

|E| = 6·5·4·5·4·6 = 14 400.

Step 3.  Translate “exactly one face is blank”  
Exactly one blank face ⇔ exactly 5 of the 6 faces receive at least one sticker.

Because F2 ,F4 ,F6 are distinct, among the remaining stickers (1,3,5) we must

• use exactly two of the three unused faces, and  
• let the third sticker duplicate an already–used face,

so that altogether 5 different faces are used.

Step 4.  Count sequences satisfying E and “exactly one blank”  

Fix any particular ordered triple (F2 ,F4 ,F6); there are 6·5·4 = 120 such triples.  
Let U be the set of the three faces not in {F2 ,F4 ,F6}.

Choose the two new faces that will be used: C(3,2)=3 possibilities.

Now assign stickers 1,3,5 subject to the restrictions F3≠F2 and F5≠F2,F4.

Case A.  One of the two new faces is used twice, the other once  
 • choose which new face is doubled: 2 ways  
 • choose which sticker gets the singleton face: 3 ways  
 • all assignments meet the restrictions  
 Total for Case A: 2·3 = 6 assignments.

Case B.  The three stickers use the two new faces once each and one old face once  
 • choose the old face (d) from {F2 ,F4 ,F6}: 3 ways  
 • count admissible permutations of {new1,new2,d}

  d = F2   : F2 may not go to 3 or 5 ⇒ 2 permutations  
  d = F4   : F4 may not go to 5   ⇒ 4 permutations  
  d = F6   : no further restriction ⇒ 6 permutations  

 Total for Case B: 2+4+6 = 12 assignments.

Thus, with the chosen (F2 ,F4 ,F6) and chosen two new faces, the number of admissible
(F1 ,F3 ,F5) triples is 6+12 = 18+36 = 54.

Therefore  

|E ∩ “exactly one blank”| = 120 · 54 = 6480.

Step 5.  Conditional probability  

p = |E ∩ “one blank”| / |E| = 6480 / 14 400 = 9 / 20.

Step 6.  Answer  

p = 9/20 = m/n with m+n = 9+20 = 29.

• \(a,b,c\) are three distinct faces,  
• \(X_k\neq a\) for \(k=3,4,5,6\),  
• \(X_k\neq b\) for \(k=5,6\).  

The domains of the remaining rolls are therefore  
\(X_1\): any of 6 faces,  
\(X_3\): any of the 5 faces other than \(a\),  
\(X_5\): any of the 4 faces other than \(a\) or \(b\).

Hence, for every ordered triple \((a,b,c)\) there are \(6\cdot5\cdot4=120\) sequences, and because there are \(6\cdot5\cdot4=120\) such triples,

\(\#B = 120 \times 120 = 14400,\qquad P(B)=\dfrac{14400}{6^{6}}=\dfrac{25}{81}.\)

Event A: exactly one face remains blank.  
Fix an ordered triple \((a,b,c)\) and let the blank face be \(d\), chosen from the 3 faces not in \(\{a,b,c\}\).  
The two remaining faces \(r,s\) (so that \(\{a,b,c,r,s\}=\{1,\dots ,6\}\setminus\{d\}\)) must each appear at least once among \(X_1,X_3,X_5\), while \(d\) must never appear.

Allowed values:

X1  ∈  \(D_1=\{1,\dots ,6\}\setminus\{d\}\)          (size 5)  
X3  ∈  \(D_3=\{1,\dots ,6\}\setminus\{a,d\}\)      (size 4)  
X5  ∈  \(D_5=\{1,\dots ,6\}\setminus\{a,b,d\}\)   (size 3)

To count those containing both \(r\) and \(s\):

• Omit \(r\): \(4\cdot3\cdot2=24\) triples  
• Omit \(s\): \(24\) triples  
• Omit both \(r,s\): \(3\cdot2\cdot1=6\) triples  

By inclusion–exclusion  
\(60-24-24+6 = 18\) triples satisfy the requirement.

Thus for the fixed \((a,b,c,d)\) there are 18 suitable sequences, and for the fixed \((a,b,c)\) (three choices for \(d\)) there are \(3\cdot18=54\) sequences.

Therefore  
\(\#(A\cap B)= 120 \times 54 = 6480,\qquad P(A\cap B)=\dfrac{6480}{6^{6}}=\dfrac{5}{36}.\)

Conditional probability

\(p=P(A\mid B)=\dfrac{P(A\cap B)}{P(B)}=\dfrac{5/36}{25/81}= \dfrac{9}{20}.\)

Step 1.  Description of the process  
After the six rolls each face of the die is

• blank if it never appeared,

(i) X2 ≠ X3,X4,X5,X6 and  
(ii) X4 ≠ X5,X6.

P(X3 ≠ X2)=5/6  
P(X4 ≠ X2)=5/6  
P(X5 ≠ X2,X4)=4/6=2/3  
P(X6 ≠ X2,X4)=4/6=2/3

Having exactly one blank means that among the six rolls

• exactly 5 distinct faces appear,  
• one face (call it D) appears twice and the other four appear once.

Step 4.  Counting sequences that satisfy both conditions

Fix F0 and D.

Case 1: X2 = D

Positions 3,4,5,6 receive the four single faces in any order.  
Count: 4! = 24.

Case 2: X2 ≠ D

{4,5} and {4,6} (otherwise X4 = X5 or X6).  
From the 10 possible pairs we exclude these 2, leaving 8 pairs.  
The four single faces (one of which goes to position 2) can be placed in 4! ways.  
Count: 8·4! = 8·24 = 192.

Total for fixed F0,D: 24+192 = 216.

With 6·5 = 30 possible (F0,D) pairs,

Number of sequences with both properties  
N(B ∧ E) = 30·216 = 6480.

Total sequences: 6^6 = 46656  
Sequences with E only: 46656·25/81 = 14400.

Hence  
p = N(B ∧ E)/N(E) = 6480/14400 = 9/20.

p = 9/20 = m/n with m+n = 9+20 = 29.

\boxed{29}

Let the six rolls of the die produce the ordered sequence of faces  

(j₁ , j₂ , j₃ , j₄ , j₅ , j₆),

where sticker k is placed on face j_k (faces are numbered 1–6 and each roll is independent and uniform).

1.  Characterising event E  
   “Stickers 2, 4 and 6 are visible”

   • j₂ , j₄ , j₆ are all on different faces.  
   • For sticker 2 to stay visible: j₃ , j₄ , j₅ , j₆ ≠ j₂.  
   • For sticker 4 to stay visible: j₅ , j₆ ≠ j₄.  

   From this we have the following choices:

        j₂ : 6 possibilities
        j₃ : 5  (≠ j₂)
        j₄ : 5  (≠ j₂)
        j₅ : 4  (≠ j₂ , j₄)
        j₆ : 4  (≠ j₂ , j₄)
        j₁ : 6  (no restriction)

   Total sequences satisfying E:

        N(E) = 6·5·5·4·4·6 = 14 400.

2.  Characterising event A  
   “Exactly one face is still blank”  
   ⇔ exactly 5 distinct faces appear in the six rolls.

3.  Counting sequences that satisfy both A and E  

   Choose the one blank face first: 6 possibilities.

   Now all six rolls must land on the remaining 5 faces, and, among those 5 faces, every one must appear at least once.  
   Denote by T₅ the number of sequences on a fixed set of 5 faces that satisfy the visibility restrictions of step 1.

   For n available faces the number of sequences satisfying the visibility restrictions is

        n² (n−1)² (n−2)²
        (j₂: n   ; j₃: n−1 ; j₄: n−1 ;
         j₅: n−2 ; j₆: n−2 ; j₁: n )

   Hence  
        T₅ = 5²·4²·3² = 3600.

   We need the sequences that actually use all 5 faces, so apply inclusion–exclusion over the 5 faces:

        total on 5 faces            :  3600
        − sequences missing 1 face  :  −5·(4²·3²·2²)   = −5·576  = −2880
        + sequences missing 2 faces :  +10·(3²·2²·1²)  = +10·36  =  +360
        (missing 3 or more faces is impossible under the restrictions)

        S = 3600 − 2880 + 360 = 1080.

   Therefore  

        N(A ∧ E) = 6 (choice of blank face) · 1080 = 6480.

4.  Conditional probability

        p = P(A | E) = N(A ∧ E) / N(E)
          = 6480 / 14 400
          = 9 / 20.

Thus  

        p = 9/20 ⇒ m = 9 , n = 20 , m + n = 29.

\boxed{29}

Let the six rolls be  

(r₁ , r₂ , r₃ , r₄ , r₅ , r₆),

where r_k is the face that is on top when sticker k is placed.  
After all six stickers are placed, the sticker that is visible on a face is the highest–numbered sticker ever placed on that face.

Event A ( “the even stickers 2,4,6 are visible” ) gives the following requirements.

1. r₂ is different from r₃ , r₄ , r₅ , r₆.  
2. r₄ is different from r₅ , r₆.  
3. r₆ is different from r₂ and r₄.  
4. Consequently r₂ , r₄ , r₆ are three distinct faces.

(No other restrictions are needed.)

--------------------------------------------------------------------
1.  Count the sequences satisfying A
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Choose the three faces that receive stickers 2,4,6:

r₂ : 6 choices  
r₄ : 5 choices (≠ r₂)  
r₆ : 4 choices (≠ r₂ , r₄)

For the remaining rolls

r₁ : no restriction → 6 choices  
r₃ : any face except r₂ → 5 choices  
r₅ : any face except r₂ , r₄ → 4 choices

Total sequences for A:

6·5·4 · 6·5·4 = 14400.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
2.  Count the sequences satisfying A and B  
    (B = “exactly one face never receives a sticker”)
--------------------------------------------------------------------
Fix a particular ordered triple (r₂ , r₄ , r₆); as above these are three distinct faces.  
Let S₀ = {r₂ , r₄ , r₆}.  
The other three faces form the set T = {a , b , c}.  
Exactly one face must stay blank, so among r₁ , r₃ , r₅ the faces a and b must appear at least once, while c must not appear.

Allowed faces for each roll (aside from the blank condition):

r₁ : any of the 5 faces in S₀ ∪ T\{c} → 5 choices  
r₃ : same 5, but cannot be r₂ → 4 choices  
r₅ : same 5, but cannot be r₂ or r₄ → 3 choices  

Hence there are 5·4·3 = 60 ordered triples (r₁ , r₃ , r₅) that avoid c.

Now impose “a and b each appear at least once”.

Number missing a: 4·3·2 = 24  
Number missing b: 24  
Number missing both a and b: 3·2·1 = 6  

By inclusion–exclusion the required number is

60 − 24 − 24 + 6 = 18.

For the fixed triple (r₂ , r₄ , r₆) there are 3 choices for which face of T is the blank, so

18 · 3 = 54

triples (r₁ , r₃ , r₅) give both A and B.

Thus the total sequences for A ∧ B are

120 (choices for r₂ , r₄ , r₆) · 54 = 6480.

--------------------------------------------------------------------
3.  Conditional probability

P(A) = 14400 / 6⁶,  P(A ∧ B) = 6480 / 6⁶.

p = P(B | A) = P(A ∧ B) / P(A) = 6480 / 14400 = 9 / 20.

Therefore m = 9, n = 20 and

\boxed{29}.

Let the six rolls of the die be the ordered sequence  

(s₁ , s₂ , s₃ , s₄ , s₅ , s₆), sᵢ ∈ {1,2,3,4,5,6}.

After all six stickers are placed, sticker k is visible exactly when no later sticker was ever put on the same physical face.

Event E ( “all even stickers are visible” ) means  
• s₂ ≠ s₃ ,s₄ ,s₅ ,s₆ (sticker 2 un-covered)  
• s₄ ≠ s₅ ,s₆      (sticker 4 un-covered)

Sticker 6 is always visible because it is the last one placed.

1.  Counting sequences that satisfy E  

For any fixed value of s₂, each of s₃,s₄,s₅,s₆ must avoid it: factor (5/6)^4.  
Given s₂, s₄ must also avoid s₂ (5/6 factor) and s₅,s₆ must avoid s₄ (4/6 each).  
Counting directly,

number of possibilities for (s₂,…,s₆) = 5·5·4·4 = 400  
(5 choices for s₃, 5 for s₄, 4 for s₅, 4 for s₆),

so total sequences satisfying E:

6 choices for s₂  ⇒ 6·400 = 2400 choices for (s₂,…,s₆)  
6 arbitrary choices for s₁ ⇒ 2400·6 = 14400 sequences.

Hence  
P(E) = 14400 / 6⁶ = 14400 / 46656 = 25/81.

2.  Adding the requirement “exactly one blank face” (event A)

Exactly one face of the die is never rolled ⇔ exactly five distinct faces appear in the six rolls.  
Thus, among the six positions there is precisely one repeated face (multiplicity pattern 2,1,1,1,1).

Let the duplicated positions be (i , j) with i < j.

Because of E:

• Any pair (2 , j) with j > 2 is forbidden (that would cover sticker 2).  
• Pairs (4 ,5) and (4 ,6) are forbidden (that would cover sticker 4).  
• Pair (2 ,4) is also impossible (s₄ ≠ s₂ is required).

The 15 position–pairs reduce to the following 9 admissible pairs:

(1,2) , (1,3) , (1,4) , (1,5) , (1,6) , (3,4) , (3,5) , (3,6) , (5,6).

For any admissible pair:

• Choose the duplicated face f – 6 possibilities.  
• Choose 4 distinct faces from the remaining 5 and assign them to the remaining 4 positions –  
  P(5,4)=5·4·3·2 = 120 possibilities.  
• Every such assignment automatically satisfies all the E–constraints (s₃..s₆ ≠ s₂ and s₅,s₆ ≠ s₄) and gives exactly one blank face.

Thus each pair contributes 6·120 = 720 sequences, and

number of sequences satisfying A ∩ E = 9 · 720 = 6480.

Therefore  

P(A ∩ E) = 6480 / 6⁶ = 6480 / 46656 = 5 / 36.

3.  Conditional probability

p = P(A | E) = P(A ∩ E) / P(E) = (5/36) ÷ (25/81) = 9 / 20.

So p = 9/20, and with p = m/n in lowest terms,

m + n = 9 + 20 = 29.

\boxed{29}
